Grammar Module — N5
I Volitional, Invitations & Modality
  1. ましょうLet's · Shall we — polite volitional
  2. ましょうかShall we · Shall I — polite volitional question
  3. ないか · ませんかWon't you · Would you like to — invitation
  4. The ましょう familyrecap
  5. てもいいIt's okay to, May · Can — permission
  6. てくださいPlease do — polite request
  7. ないでくださいPlease don't — polite negative request
  8. なければいけない ·なければいけませんMust · Have to — obligation
  9. てはいけない ·てはいけませんMust not · May not — prohibition
  10. たほうがいい ·ないほうがいいYou should · You'd better — advice
  11. The modality stackrecap
N5
ましょう
(~mashou)
Let's · Shall we — polite volitional
Verb stem + ましょう
About

This grammar structure is used to suggest or propose doing something together. It can also serve as a personal declaration of intent rather than a suggestion to another person, expressing strong resolve — similar to "I shall" in English.

It is used exclusively in polite speech and has no casual equivalent. ましょう is a conjugation of the auxiliary verb ます, and always attaches to the verb stem.

To use ましょう, take the verb's verb stem (remove ます from the polite present form) and attach ましょう directly.

Examplesshow ▶
モールに行きましょう！
Let's go to the mall!
energetic shared proposal; ましょう expresses collective will
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
映画を見ましょう。
Let's watch a movie.
softer joint suggestion; less pushy than てください
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう帰りましょう。
Let's head home now.
もう adds a sense of 'it's time now'; nudges the group to wrap up
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
はい、行きましょう！
Yes, let's go!
echoing the same verb back when agreeing is natural and enthusiastic
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
５キロ痩せましょう！
I will lose 5 kg! (I shall do it!)
self-directed volitional; used as a personal declaration, not an invitation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
N5
ましょうか
(~mashou ka)
Shall we · Shall I — polite volitional question
Verb stem + ましょうか
About

This grammar structure is used to suggest a shared action as an open question, or to offer to do something for someone. Depending on context, it translates as either "Shall we...?" or "Shall I...?".

It is used exclusively in polite speech and always attaches to the verb stem.

ましょうか is formed by adding the question particle か to ましょう. This turns the assertive volitional into a direct question — softer and more tentative than ましょう alone, and open to the other person's response.

To use ましょうか, take the verb's verb stem (remove ます from the polite present form) and attach ましょうか directly.

Examplesshow ▶
レストランまで車で行きましょうか？
Shall we go to the restaurant by car?
offers a specific logistical plan; listener expected to agree or counter
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今夜走りましょうか？
Shall we run tonight?
lighter joint suggestion; inviting someone to share an activity tonight
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
一緒にイタリアに行きましょうか？
Shall we go to Italy together?
tentative long-range plan; 一緒に flags it as shared
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
手伝いましょうか？
Shall I help you?
offering to do something for someone else; 'shall I' = speaker would perform the action
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
ドアを開けましょうか？
Shall I open the door?
small polite service offer; common in formal or hospitality settings
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
N5
ないか · ませんか
(~nai ka · ~masen ka)
Won't you · Would you like to — invitation
Polite
Verb stem + ませんか
Casual
Verb [ない]negative form
+ か
About

This grammar structure is used to extend a polite invitation or suggest doing something together. Despite its negative grammatical form, ませんか carries a positive, inviting meaning — closer to "Would you like to...?" or "Why don't we...?" in English.

It is used exclusively in polite speech and always attaches to the verb stem. Unlike ましょうか, which proposes a shared action or offers to do something, ませんか specifically extends an invitation to the other person — making it the more considerate and socially appropriate choice when suggesting plans.

To use ませんか, take the verb's verb stem (remove ます from the polite present form) and attach ませんか directly.

The casual equivalent is ないか — the "Wanna?" of English. Formed by taking the verb's negative plain form (ない-form (negative form)) and adding か, it carries the same inviting nuance but is used only in informal settings with close friends, family, or peers of equal standing. It would sound out of place or rude with anyone you are not on familiar terms with.

Examplesshow ▶
明日は温泉に行きませんか？
Won't you go to the onsen with me tomorrow?
classic polite invitation; warmer and less presumptuous than ましょうか
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
一緒にお祭りへ行きませんか？
Would you like to go to the festival together?
framing an outing as an invitation rather than a proposal
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
いっしょに昼ごはんを食べませんか？
Would you like to eat lunch together?
very common workplace or school phrase for lunch invitations
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
映画を見ませんか？
Would you like to watch a movie?
minimal invitation; open to whoever is present
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今晩うちで晩ごはんを食べませんか？
Would you like to have dinner at my place tonight?
hosting invitation; the most personal variant — come to my place
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
一緒に公園に行かないか？
Wanna go to the park together?
casual masculine form; used among close male friends or peers of equal standing
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
何か食べないか？
Wanna grab something to eat?
very casual rhetorical offer; expecting the other person to want food too
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
Quick Recap
The ましょう family
ましょう · ましょうか · ませんか · ないか
Comparison
Form Register Function Feels like
ましょう Polite Assert a shared action or personal resolve "Let's!" / "I shall!"
ましょうか Polite Suggest as an open question; offer to do something for someone "Shall we?" / "Shall I?"
ませんか Polite Invite the other person to do something "Would you like to?"
ないか Casual Same as ませんか, but informal "Wanna?"
When to use what
You're suggesting something and assuming the other person is in → ましょう
You're suggesting something but leaving it open, or offering to do something for someone → ましょうか
You're inviting someone and putting the choice in their hands → ませんか
Any of the above, but with a close friend or family member → ないか
N5
てもいい
(~te mo ii · ~te mo ii desu)
It's okay to, May · Can — permission
Polite
Verb
Verb [て]te form
+ もいいです
い-adjective
い-adj [い] + く + てもいいです
な-adjective
な-adj + でもいいです
Noun
Noun + でもいいです
Casual
Verb
Verb [て]te form
+ もいい
い-adjective
い-adj [い] + く + てもいい
な-adjective
な-adj + でもいい
Noun
Noun + でもいい
About

This grammar structure is used to give or ask for permission to do something — equivalent to "you may," "it's okay to," or "can I?" in English. Adding か turns it into a permission request: てもいいですか ("Is it okay if I...?" · "May I...?").

For verbs, attach もいい to the て-form. Some verbs use で instead of て in the て-form — this follows normal て-form conjugation rules and carries through into this construction.

For い-adjectives, drop the final い, add く, then attach てもいい: for example, 遅い → 遅く → 遅くてもいい. For な-adjectives and nouns, attach でもいい directly to the stem — the で here is the conjunctive form of the copula, not the て-form.

In casual speech, も is sometimes dropped — ていい or でいい — carrying the same meaning but sounding more direct. Keep the も unless you know the listener well.

Examplesshow ▶
疲れたら、先に帰ってもいいです。
If you're tired, it's okay to leave early.
permissive; granting someone leave to go early without needing to feel bad
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
明日の会議を欠席してもいいですか？
May I skip tomorrow's meeting?
politely requesting exemption from an obligation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
少し遅くてもいいです。
It's okay if you're a little late.
reassuring someone that a slight imperfection is fine; い-adj + てもいい
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
このサイズは少し大きくてもいいですか？
Is it okay if this size is a little big?
checking if a marginal fit is acceptable
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
服装はカジュアルでもいいです。
Casual attire is fine.
dress code guidance; な-adj / noun + でもいい for accepting alternatives
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
料理は簡単でもいいですか？
Is it okay if the food is simple?
confirming minimal effort is acceptable; setting a low bar intentionally
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
支払いはカードでもいいです。
Payment by card is fine.
confirming payment method is accepted; noun + でもいい
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
参加者は一人でもいいですか？
Is it okay if there's only one participant?
checking if solo participation counts; confirming a minimum is enough
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
もう寝てもいい。
It's okay to go to sleep now.
self-permission in plain form; checking if it's fine to retire for the night
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
ちょっと先に食べてもいい？
Can I start eating first?
casual go-ahead to start before everyone is ready
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
多少古くてもいい、使えれば。
It's okay if it's somewhat old, as long as it works.
practical trade-off thinking; function matters more than age
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
このジャケット、少し大きくてもいい？
Is it okay if this jacket is a little big?
casual sizing tolerance check
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
字は下手でもいい、気持ちが伝わればいい。
It's okay if your handwriting is bad — what matters is that the feeling comes across.
sincere message: the feeling matters more than execution quality
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
この店、ちょっと地味でもいい？
Is it okay if this place is a bit plain?
checking if a plain or unglamorous venue is acceptable
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
飲み物はお茶でもいい。
Tea is fine for a drink.
accepting a simpler alternative without complaint
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
待ち合わせ、駅でもいい？
Is the station okay for meeting up?
casual confirmation of a meetup location
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
てください
(~te kudasai)
Please do — polite request
Verb [て]te form
+ ください
About

This grammar structure is used to politely ask or instruct someone to do something. It is the standard way to make a request in Japanese, equivalent to "please do [something]" in English.

ください is the imperative form of くださる, the honorific equivalent of くれる ("to give"). In this construction it functions simply as "please do" — always attaching to the て-form of a verb, never the dictionary or ます-form.

To use てください, conjugate the verb into its て-form and attach ください directly.

In casual speech, ください can be dropped entirely, leaving just the て-form as a softened request. This is common with close friends and family, and works for both genders.

Examplesshow ▶
ちょっと待ってください。
Please wait a moment.
ubiquitous polite request; ちょっと softens the imposition
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
ドアを開けてください。
Please open the door.
direct instruction phrased politely; typical in service or guidance contexts
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
名前を書いてください。
Please write your name.
standard form-filling or registration instruction
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう一度言ってください。
Please say it again.
asking for repetition; essential phrase in language learning contexts
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
ここに座ってください。
Please sit here.
directing someone to a specific seat; hospitality or classroom context
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
ちょっと待って。
Wait a sec.
casual te-form request; works as an informal imperative between close people
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
テレビを消して。
Turn off the TV.
domestic plain request; te-form alone between close family or friends
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
N5
ないでください
(~naide kudasai)
Please don't — polite negative request
Verb [ない]negative form
+ でください
About

This grammar structure is used to politely ask someone not to do something. It is the negative equivalent of てください.

Despite the visual similarity to てください, the で here does not come from the て-form. It attaches to the negative plain form (ない-form (negative form)) of a verb, making it a structurally distinct construction.

To use ないでください, take the verb's ない-form (negative form) (negative plain form) and attach でください directly.

In casual speech, ください can be dropped, leaving just ないで as a softened negative request. Used with close friends and family.

Examplesshow ▶
ここで写真を撮らないでください。
Please don't take pictures here.
polite public space prohibition; posted notices and verbal warnings
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
大きい声で話さないでください。
Please don't speak in a loud voice.
venue-specific etiquette; quiet zones, libraries, hospitals
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
宿題を忘れないでください。
Please don't forget your homework.
classroom instruction; reminder rather than a rule
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あそこでタバコを吸わないでください。
Please don't smoke over there.
directing someone away from a specific zone
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
この部屋に入らないでください。
Please don't enter this room.
clear access restriction; often on signs or from authority
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
見ないで。
Don't look.
casual plain; asking for privacy; direct between close people
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
忘れないで。
Don't forget.
casual reminder; gentle or slightly urgent depending on tone
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
なければいけない ·
なければいけません
(~nakereba ikenai · ~nakereba ikemasen)
Must · Have to — obligation
Polite
VERB [ない]negative form
+ ければいけません
Casual
VERB [ない]negative form
+ ければいけない
Very casual
VERB [ない]negative form
+ きゃ
About

This grammar structure is used to express obligation — that something must or has to be done. It implies that not doing it would cause trouble, inconvenience, or be wrong.

Despite its negative grammatical form, this structure carries a positive meaning — "must do," not "must not do." Both forms are built from the verb's ない-form (negative form): drop the final い, then attach ければいけない (casual) or ければいけません (polite).

To use なければいけない · なければいけません, take the verb's ない-form (negative form), drop the final い, and attach ければいけない or ければいけません directly.

The very casual contraction なきゃ carries the same meaning and is extremely common in spoken Japanese — the equivalent of "gotta" in English. It is formed the same way, replacing ければいけない entirely with きゃ.

Examplesshow ▶
パスポートを更新しなければいけません。
I must renew my passport.
formal obligation; civic or administrative duty framing
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
ゴミを分別しなければいけません。
We have to separate our trash.
rule-based obligation; trash separation is strictly observed in Japan
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日は９時に寝なければいけません。
I have to go to sleep at 9 tonight.
self-imposed or imposed bedtime; polite framing
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
来週までに引っ越しの準備をしなければいけない。
I have to get ready for the move by next week.
time-bound obligation in plain form; internal pressure or planning
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
世界史の本を読まなければいけない。
I need to read a world-history book.
study obligation driven by a task list
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
毎日運動しなければいけない。
I have to exercise every day.
health-related self-obligation; daily routine goal
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
電気代を払わなきゃ。
Gotta pay the electric bill.
very casual contraction of なければならない; colloquial everyday reminder
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE VERY CASUAL
今日はスーパーに行かなきゃ。
Gotta go to the supermarket today.
informal to-do list phrasing; most common register for internal obligations
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE VERY CASUAL
勉強しなきゃ。
Gotta study.
the shortest casual form; acknowledging a duty without ceremony
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE VERY CASUAL
N5
てはいけない ·
てはいけません
(~te wa ikenai · ~te wa ikemasen)
Must not · May not — prohibition
Polite
Verb [て]te form
+ はいけません
Casual
Verb [て]te form
+ はいけない
Very casual
Verb [て]te form
+ ちゃte/じゃdeいけない
About

This grammar structure is used to prohibit or strongly forbid an action — expressing that something must not or is not allowed to be done. The は in this construction is the topic particle, pronounced wa, not ha.

Both forms attach to the て-form of a verb. は is the topic particle, and いけない · いけません means "it won't do · it's not acceptable." Together they convey: doing X is not acceptable. There is no double negative — the construction is negative in form but singular in meaning.

To use てはいけません · てはいけない, conjugate the verb into its て-form and attach はいけません or はいけない directly. Note that if the verb's て-form ends in で rather than て, the construction becomes ではいけません · ではいけない accordingly.

In casual speech, ては and では both contract by dropping は: ては becomes ちゃ, and では becomes じゃ. Which form appears depends purely on the verb's て-form — if it ends in て, use ちゃいけない; if it ends in で, use じゃいけない. The word だめ goes one step further, dropping いけない entirely and expressing the same prohibition in the most casual register.

Examplesshow ▶
ここでタバコを吸ってはいけません。
You must not smoke here.
public prohibition announcement; rule enforcement register
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
携帯電話を使ってはいけません。
You may not use your phone.
restricted zone instruction; common in cinemas, hospitals, exam halls
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
学校でお酒を飲んではいけません。
You must not drink alcohol at school.
institutional rule with clear social context
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
このドアを開けてはいけない。
Do not open this door.
plain prohibition; direct and firm, slightly formal
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
夜に電話してはいけない。
You must not call at night.
social etiquette rule about calling hours
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
ここで遊んではいけない。
You must not play here.
area-based restriction; commonly used for children or posted signs
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
危ない場所に行っちゃいけない。
You must not go to dangerous places.
〜ちゃ is a casual contraction of 〜ては; parental or concerned-friend register
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE VERY CASUAL
嘘をついちゃいけない。
You must not tell lies.
moral prohibition in casual speech; teaching right from wrong
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE VERY CASUAL
旗を踏んじゃいけない。
You must not step on a flag.
〜んじゃ contracts 〜ではいけない slightly differently; both are casual equivalents
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE VERY CASUAL
人のものを盗んじゃいけない。
You must not steal other people's things.
strong moral prohibition in very casual register
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE VERY CASUAL
N5
たほうがいい ·
ないほうがいい
(~ta hou ga ii · ~nai hou ga ii)
You should · You'd better — advice
Affirmative
Polite
Verb [た]past form
+ ほうがいいです
Casual
Verb [た]past form
+ ほうがいい
Negative
Polite
Verb [ない]negative form
+ ほうがいいです
Casual
Verb [ない]negative form
+ ほうがいい
About

This grammar structure is used to give direct advice or make a strong suggestion — implying that not following it may lead to negative consequences.

The verb form is fixed and determined by polarity, not by tense. Affirmative advice always takes the た-form (past form); negative advice always takes the present ない-form (negative form) — never the past negative なかった. This is one of the most common mistakes with this structure.

方 (ほう) literally means "way" or "direction" — the phrase suggests that one specific course of action is simply the better way. Using the dictionary form instead of the た-form (past form) is possible but shifts the meaning from direct advice to general opinion.

To use ほうがいい, attach the verb's た-form (past form) + ほうがいい(です) for affirmative advice, or the verb's ない-form (negative form) + ほうがいい(です) for negative advice.

Examplesshow ▶
もっと野菜を食べたほうがいいです。
You'd better eat more vegetables.
health advice; past form of the verb is standard in affirmative ほうがいい
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
早く寝たほうがいいです。
You should go to bed early.
friendly advice; past form slightly softens it compared to a direct command
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
授業を休まないほうがいいです。
It's better not to skip classes.
negative advice; plain negative verb + ほうがいい
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
夜遅くまで起きないほうがいいです。
You'd better not stay up late.
discouraging a bad habit; negative form + ほうがいい
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
薬を飲んだほうがいいよ。
You should take the medicine.
casual advice with よ; softer and more personal than the polite version
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
警察に言ったほうがいい。
It would be better to tell the police.
advice in a serious or high-stakes situation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
そんなこと言わないほうがいいよ。
You'd better not say something like that.
casual caution against saying something offensive or hurtful
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
ここに来ないほうがいい。
It's better not to come here.
advice to stay away; could be for safety, awkwardness, or both
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
Quick Recap
The modality stack
てもいい · てください · ないでください · なければいけない · てはいけない · たほうがいい
Comparison
Form Register Function Feels like
〜てもいい Polite Seek or grant permission "May I?" / "You can"
〜てください Polite Politely request someone to do something "Please do"
〜ないでください Polite Politely ask someone not to do something "Please don't"
〜なければいけない Polite Express obligation — no real choice "Have to / Must"
〜てはいけない Polite Express prohibition — not allowed "Must not / Can't"
〜たほうがいい Polite Give soft advice or recommendation "You should / You'd better"
When to use what
Asking if something is okay, or telling someone they can → 〜てもいい
Telling someone to do something politely → 〜てください
Telling someone not to do something politely → 〜ないでください
Something is obligatory — external pressure, duty, or rule → 〜なければいけない
Something is forbidden — a rule or social norm prohibits it → 〜てはいけない
Giving advice without forcing it — a recommendation, not an order → 〜たほうがいい
II Copulas, Existence & State
  1. だAm · Is · Are — plain copula
  2. ですAm · Is · Are — polite copula
  3. だろうProbably · I think — casual supposition
  4. でしょうProbably · I suppose — polite supposition
  5. んです · のですThe fact is, You see, It's because — explanatory · emphatic
  6. The copula familyrecap
  7. があるThere is, Have — existence of inanimate things
  8. がいるThere is, Have — existence of living things
  9. になる · くなるTo become — change of state
N5
だ
(da)
Am · Is · Are — plain copula
な-adjective
な-adj + だ
Noun
Noun + だ
About

This grammar structure is used to identify or describe something in plain speech — the casual equivalent of です, translating as "am," "is," or "are" in English.

だ follows nouns and な-adjectives directly. It cannot be used with い-adjectives — this is one of the most important rules about だ. The plain form of an い-adjective already carries the meaning of "is" on its own: 大きい means "it is big" without any copula attached. Never add だ after an い-adjective.

だ is used exclusively in casual speech. In polite speech, です takes its place. While the two are often treated as direct equivalents, they are not interchangeable — だ will never appear in formal or polite sentences.

Conjugation: だ changes form for tense and polarity. Past affirmative becomes だった. Present negative becomes じゃない (contracted from ではない — the latter is more formal). Past negative combines both: じゃなかった (drop い from じゃない, add かった). These four forms — だ · じゃない · だった · じゃなかった — cover every tense and polarity combination.

Questions: だ does not have a dedicated question form — to ask a question in casual speech, either add か directly after it or drop it and use rising intonation instead. Adding か sounds blunt and is typically associated with masculine speech; rising intonation is softer and more common in everyday conversation. In polite speech, ですか takes over this role entirely and is the standard way to ask questions.

Conjugation — all forms
RegisterTenseAffirmativeNegative
PlainPresentだじゃない・ではない
PlainPastだったじゃなかった・ではなかった
PolitePresentですじゃないです・ではないです・ではありません
PolitePastでしたじゃなかったです・ではなかったです・ではありませんでした
Examplesshow ▶
このカフェは静かだ。
This café is quiet.
plain casual affirmative; だ at sentence-end is neutral to slightly masculine
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
今日は全然暇じゃない。
I'm not free at all today.
casual plain negative; じゃない is the everyday spoken form
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
子供の頃、勉強が嫌いだった。
I hated studying when I was a kid.
casual past; expressing a strong past dislike
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
姉は昔、料理が上手じゃなかった。
My older sister wasn't good at cooking before.
casual past negative; talking informally about someone else's past
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの人は医者だ。
That person is a doctor.
plain noun assertion; casual and direct
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
これは私の財布じゃない。
This isn't my wallet.
casual denial of ownership; everyday speech
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
昨日は母の誕生日だった。
Yesterday was my mother's birthday.
casual past copula; noun/な-adj + だった
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
去年の今頃、私はまだ中学生じゃなかった。
Around this time last year, I wasn't a middle school student yet.
timeline clarification in plain; 'at that point I wasn't yet...'
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
です
(desu)
Am · Is · Are — polite copula
い-adjective
い-adj spoken / daily use
+ です
な-adjective
な-adj + です
Noun
Noun + です
About

This grammar structure is used to identify or describe something in polite speech — the polite equivalent of だ, translating as "am," "is," or "are" in English.

です attaches to nouns and な-adjectives in the same way as だ. Unlike だ, it also attaches to い-adjectives to form polite predicates — this is the standard way to make an い-adjective polite in everyday speech. In strictly formal writing, い-adjective + です is sometimes considered incorrect, but in daily spoken Japanese and texting it is completely normal and you will use it constantly.

です and だ are often described as polite and casual versions of each other, but they are standalone structures — not simply interchangeable forms of the same word.

Conjugation: です changes form for tense and polarity. Past affirmative becomes でした. Present negative becomes じゃないです (contracted from ではないです — ではありません is the more formal variant). Past negative combines both: じゃなかったです (drop い from じゃないです, add かったです). These four forms — です · じゃないです · でした · じゃなかったです — cover every tense and polarity combination.

Questions: Append か to any of the four forms to turn a statement into a question: ですか · じゃないですか · でしたか · じゃなかったですか. Intonation rises naturally on the final か.

Conjugation — all forms
RegisterTenseAffirmativeNegative
PlainPresentだじゃない・ではない
PlainPastだったじゃなかった・ではなかった
PolitePresentですじゃないです・ではないです・ではありません
PolitePastでしたじゃなかったです・ではなかったです・ではありませんでした
Examplesshow ▶
このかばんは重いです。
This bag is heavy.
basic polite い-adj assertion; most neutral and common
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この荷物、重いですか？
Is this luggage heavy?
polite question; topic sometimes marked by comma in speech rhythm
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
駅から学校まであまり遠くないです。
The school isn't very far from the station.
polite negative い-adj; あまり softens the negation
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
この道、そんなに遠くないですか？
Is this road not that far?
seeking confirmation with そんなに; 'not that far, right?'
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日のテストは思ったより易しかったです。
Yesterday's test was easier than I expected.
past polite い-adj; 思ったより = 'more/less than I expected'
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先週の試験、難しかったですか？
Was last week's exam difficult?
asking for past experience or opinion about an event
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週の夏祭りはあまり楽しくなかったです。
Last week's summer festival wasn't very fun.
polite past negative hedged with あまり; mild disappointment
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日の映画、あまり面白くなかったですか？
Wasn't yesterday's film very interesting?
negative question checking shared disappointment
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
隣の部屋は静かです。
The next room is quiet.
polite な-adj present; common in hospitality or housing descriptions
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この辺は夜も安全ですか？
Is this area safe at night too?
checking safety at a specific time; 夜も = 'even at night'
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は全然暇じゃないです。
I'm not free at all today.
polite negative; じゃないです is a common casual-polite alternative to ではありません
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あの店、あまり有名じゃないですか？
Isn't that shop very well-known?
confirming relative obscurity of a place
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
子供の頃、犬がとても好きでした。
I liked dogs a lot when I was a child.
past polite な-adj; 好き takes が, not を
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
学生の頃、数学が得意でしたか？
Were you good at maths when you were a student?
past ability question; 得意 also takes が
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
小学校の先生はあまり親切じゃなかったです。
My primary school teacher wasn't very kind.
past polite negative hedged with あまり; common childhood memory phrasing
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昔の職場、そんなに好きじゃなかったですか？
Didn't you like your old workplace that much?
checking shared experience; slightly invites commiseration
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
父は医者です。
My father is a doctor.
basic noun + です; introducing someone's profession
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんはこのクラスの学生ですか？
Is Tanaka-san a student in this class?
confirming affiliation or identity
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
これは私の本じゃないです。
This is not my book.
polite possession denial; clear and simple
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あの方、先生じゃないですか？
Isn't that person a teacher?
あの方 is a formal way to refer to someone; seeking confirmation with surprise
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週の土曜日は弟の誕生日でした。
Last Saturday was my younger brother's birthday.
past copula でした for dates and occasions
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日は会議の日でしたか？
Was yesterday the day of the meeting?
confirming if a date was designated for a specific event
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
去年の今頃、私はまだ高校生じゃなかったです。
Around this time last year, I wasn't a high school student yet.
past negative clarifying a timeline; 'I wasn't yet...'
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
その頃、まだ社員じゃなかったですか？
Weren't you an employee yet at that point?
checking past employment status at a specific time
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
N5
だろう
(~darou)
Probably · I think — casual supposition
Verb
Verbdictionary form
+ だろう
い-adjective
い-adjdictionary form
+ だろう
な-adjective
な-adj no な
+ だろう
Noun
Noun + だろう
About

This grammar structure is used to express probability or supposition in casual speech — the plain/casual equivalent of でしょう. It translates as "probably," "I think," or "right?" in English.

だろう is the presumptive form of だ, the plain copula. For verbs and い-adjectives, it follows the dictionary form directly. For な-adjectives, attach だろう to the adjective stem — do not add な before it. For nouns, attach だろう directly.

Because だ is more casual than です, だろう carries a more casual or masculine tone than でしょう. It is often used by men or in contexts where a more assertive tone is intended.

With a rising intonation, だろう can contract to だろ, functioning as a rhetorical confirmation — seeking agreement from the listener, like "right?" in English.

Examplesshow ▶
彼はもう家に帰っているだろう。
He's probably already home.
present probability in casual plain masculine register; common in reflective speech
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
弟はまだ起きていないだろう。
My younger brother is probably not up yet.
negative probability; implies the person is a habitual late sleeper
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
昨日の試合、父は見ただろう。
Dad probably watched yesterday's game.
past probability about a third party's action; no direct evidence
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
先生はその話を聞かなかっただろう。
The teacher probably didn't hear about that.
past negative probability; speaker assumes teacher was kept out of the loop
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
今の時間、外は寒いだろう。
It's probably cold outside at this hour.
environmental assumption based on the current time and season
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
駅まであまり遠くないだろう。
It's probably not that far to the station.
spatial probability to reassure someone about distance
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
夏休み、楽しかっただろう。
Summer vacation was probably fun.
empathetic assumption about someone else's enjoyable experience
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日のテスト、そんなに難しくなかっただろう。
Yesterday's test was probably not that hard.
softened past assumption; perhaps hoping they agree it wasn't hard
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
今日は姉も暇だろう。
My older sister is probably free today too.
including another person in a probability statement
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
あの部屋はそんなに静かじゃないだろう。
That room is probably not that quiet.
assuming a negative quality about a place based on context
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
子供の頃、この辺りは有名だっただろう。
This area was probably well-known when we were kids.
speculating about a historical state of a location
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
あいつ、高校の時は真面目じゃなかっただろう。
He was probably not serious in high school.
casual past character assessment with slightly negative tone
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの人は警察だろう。
That person is probably a police officer.
speculation about someone's identity based on appearance or context
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
これは田中さんの傘じゃないだろう。
This is probably not Tanaka's umbrella.
probabilistic denial of ownership
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
その頃、あいつはまだ学生だっただろう。
He was probably still a student back then.
past status probability; filling in a timeline for someone
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昔、ここはレストランじゃなかっただろう。
This place probably wasn't a restaurant before.
past historical assumption about a space
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
でしょう
(~deshou)
Probably · I suppose — polite supposition
Verb
Verbdictionary form
+ でしょう
い-adjective
い-adjdictionary form
+ でしょう
な-adjective
な-adj no だ・な
+ でしょう
Noun
Noun no だ
+ でしょう
About

This grammar structure is used to express a guess, prediction, or inference about something — equivalent to "probably," "I suppose," or "I think" in English. It can also be used to soften an assertion or hedge an opinion politely.

でしょう is the presumptive form of です — its plain/casual equivalent is だろう. For verbs and い-adjectives, attach でしょう to the dictionary form directly. For な-adjectives, attach to the adjective stem — do not add な or だ before it. For nouns, attach directly — do not add だ.

Adding か gives でしょうか — a polite way to ask for someone's guess or opinion, softer than ですか.

In casual speech, でしょ with a rising intonation works as a rhetorical confirmation — like "right?" in English.

Examplesshow ▶
あしたは雨が降るでしょう。
It will probably rain tomorrow.
weather forecast register; でしょう sounds like broadcast Japanese
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼は今頃もう起きているでしょうか？
Has he probably woken up by now?
polite wondering aloud; でしょうか is softer than だろうか
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あしたは雨が降らないでしょう。
It probably won't rain tomorrow.
negative probability in polite form
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
田中さん、今日は来ないでしょうか？
Won't Tanaka-san probably come today?
polite uncertainty about someone's attendance
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼はもう家に帰ったでしょう。
He has probably gone home by now.
past probability in polite; used when wondering about someone's whereabouts
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう試験は終わったでしょうか？
Has the exam probably finished by now?
polite wondering if a scheduled event has concluded
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
田中さんはその話を聞かなかったでしょう。
Tanaka-san probably didn't hear about it.
polite past negative probability; diplomatic way to speculate
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
彼女はそのことを知らなかったでしょうか？
Probably didn't she know about it?
polite speculation about someone else's ignorance
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
北海道は今、寒いでしょう。
Hokkaido is probably cold right now.
regional weather assumption; common topic in polite small talk
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日の試験、難しいでしょうか？
Is today's exam probably difficult?
polite rhetorical wondering; slightly nervous anticipation about a test
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この問題はそんなに難しくないでしょう。
This problem probably isn't that difficult.
reassuring probability statement; teacher-to-student or colleague register
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
その道、そんなに遠くないでしょうか？
That road probably isn't that far, is it?
polite confirmation-seeking with hedged language
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日の試合は面白かったでしょう。
Yesterday's match was probably interesting.
assuming someone had an enjoyable experience; empathetic register
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日の試合、面白かったでしょうか？
Was yesterday's match probably interesting?
seeking confirmation about a shared past experience
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日はそんなに寒くなかったでしょう。
It probably wasn't that cold yesterday.
polite assumption about conditions you didn't personally experience
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
先週はそんなに忙しくなかったでしょうか？
Probably wasn't last week that busy?
polite speculation about someone else's schedule or workload
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あのレストランは有名でしょう。
That restaurant is probably well-known.
polite assumption about a restaurant's fame
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの地域は今も静かでしょうか？
Is that area probably still quiet?
checking if a quality (quietness) is still maintained over time
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あの地域はそんなに便利じゃないでしょう。
That area probably isn't that convenient.
polite assumption about inconvenience in a specific area
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あの店、そんなに有名じゃないでしょうか？
That shop probably isn't that well-known, is it?
polite checking for shared understanding of obscurity
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昔、この辺りはにぎやかだったでしょう。
This area was probably lively in the past.
historical atmosphere assumption; common nostalgic phrasing
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
その頃、彼女は日本語が得意だったでしょうか？
Was she probably good at Japanese back then?
respectful speculation about past ability
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昔はこの町はそんなに有名じゃなかったでしょう。
This town probably wasn't that well-known in the past.
past negative probability about a place's reputation
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
あの頃はそんなに便利じゃなかったでしょうか？
Probably wasn't it that convenient back then?
checking shared assumption about past living conditions
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あの人は先生でしょう。
That person is probably a teacher.
identifying someone's probable role; noun + でしょう
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんはあの会社の社員でしょうか？
Is Tanaka-san probably an employee at that company?
polite speculation about someone's professional affiliation
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あれは本物じゃないでしょう。
That is probably not the real thing.
politely doubting authenticity; softer than a direct accusation
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あれは本物じゃないでしょうか？
That is probably not real, is it?
inviting agreement on a doubt; similar to a tag question
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あの頃、彼女は学生だったでしょう。
She was probably a student back then.
past status assumption about a third party
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昔、あの人は先生だったでしょうか？
Was that person probably a teacher before?
polite inquiry about someone's past role
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
その頃、田中さんはまだ社員じゃなかったでしょう。
Tanaka-san probably wasn't an employee yet back then.
past negative status assumption in polite register
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
その頃、彼はまだ学生じゃなかったでしょうか？
Probably wasn't he a student yet back then?
checking probability of past non-student status politely
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
N5
んです · のです
(~n desu · ~no desu)
The fact is, You see, It's because — explanatory · emphatic
Polite
Verb
Verbplain form
+ んです
い-adjective
い-adjplain form
+ んです
な-adjective
な-adj + なんです
Noun
Noun + なんです
Formal / Written
Verb
Verbplain form
+ のです
い-adjective
い-adjplain form
+ のです
な-adjective
な-adj + なのです
Noun
Noun + なのです
Casual
Verb
Verbplain form
+ んだ
い-adjective
い-adjplain form
+ んだ
な-adjective
な-adj + なんだ
Noun
Noun + なんだ
About

This grammar structure is used to explain, justify, or provide background context — implying "the reason is," "you see," or "the fact is." Rather than simply reporting information, it signals that the statement is connected to something the listener has observed, asked about, or reacted to. In questions, んですか prompts the listener to explain themselves.

んです never conjugates itself. Tense and polarity come from the word before it — attach んです to any plain form, present, past, or negative. For verbs and い-adjectives, attach directly to the plain form. For な-adjectives and nouns, add な before んです.

ん is a contraction of の. The original form is のです — technically more correct, and used in formal or written Japanese, but can sound stiff in speech. んです is the standard spoken form. In casual speech it contracts further to んだ.

Do not use んです for every sentence — only when there is context, emotion, or justification involved. Overuse sounds unnatural and comes across as over-explaining.

Examplesshow ▶
今日は友達と会うんです。
I'm meeting a friend today (that's why).
giving context for a behavior or absence; the んです carries 'there's a reason for this'
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
何か急なことがあったんですか？
Did something urgent come up?
asking for the story behind an observable situation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は学校に行かないんです。
I'm not going to school today (there's a reason).
explaining current non-action with implied reason
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
どうして今日は学校に来ないんですか？
Why aren't you coming to school today?
asking for the underlying reason; softer and more empathetic than bare どうして
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
ここに財布を忘れたんです。
I left my wallet here (that's the problem).
explaining why you came back or why you're distressed
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日、何があったんですか？
What happened yesterday?
seeking explanation behind someone's current visible state
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
電車が来なかったんです。
The train didn't come (that's why I'm late).
offering the reason for lateness; very common explanation phrase
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
どうして昨日来なかったんですか？
Why didn't you come yesterday?
softer inquiry inviting someone to share context rather than demanding a reason
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
このカメラ、すごく高いんです。
This camera is really expensive (I'm explaining).
providing context for showing something; the んです invites engagement
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
体の調子が悪いんですか？
Are you feeling unwell?
reading visible cues and gently asking for the explanation
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この仕事はあまり難しくないんです。
This job isn't that difficult (actually).
correcting a misperception with an explanatory tone
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
本当にそんなに難しくないんですか？
Is it really not that difficult?
disbelieving confirmation; 'is it really that easy?'
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日のテストはとても難しかったんです。
Yesterday's test was really difficult (let me tell you).
providing context for poor results or a complaint
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日のテスト、本当に難しかったんですか？
Was yesterday's test really that difficult?
seeking confirmation with a dramatic element
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週はそんなに忙しくなかったんです。
Last week wasn't that busy (surprisingly).
correcting an overestimate with explanatory nuance
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
先週、本当にそんなに忙しくなかったんですか？
Was last week really not that busy?
politely skeptical of the downplayed claim
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼女は料理が得意なんです。
She's good at cooking (you know).
providing useful background information about a third party
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの人、本当に有名なんですか？
Is that person really famous?
surprised or skeptical recognition inquiry
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あの店はあまり有名じゃないんです。
That shop isn't very well-known (actually).
sharing insider knowledge; 'actually [you know]...'
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
本当にあの店、有名じゃないんですか？
Is that shop really not well-known?
surprised disconfirmation of assumed fame
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昔、この辺りはとても静かだったんです。
This area used to be very quiet (back then).
explaining how a place used to feel; nostalgic context-setting
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昔、本当にこの辺りは静かだったんですか？
Was this area really quiet before?
hard to believe; seeking confirmation of the past state
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昔は日本語がそんなに得意じゃなかったんです。
I wasn't that good at Japanese before (truth be told).
providing background to explain current skill level
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
本当に昔は得意じゃなかったんですか？
Were you really not good at it before?
disbelief at someone's self-deprecation
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
田中さんは医者なんです。
Tanaka-san is actually a doctor.
explaining who someone is with a revelatory nuance; 'it turns out / by the way'
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さん、実は医者なんですか？
Is Tanaka-san actually a doctor?
surprised discovery; 実は reinforces the reveal element
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は休みじゃないんです。
Today isn't a day off (that's why I'm working).
explaining why you're present or available when expected otherwise
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
本当に今日は休みじゃないんですか？
Is today really not a day off?
confirming the surprising absence of an expected day off
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日は誕生日だったんです。
Yesterday was my birthday (so I was celebrating).
explaining a celebration or explaining lateness due to it
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日、本当に誕生日だったんですか？
Was yesterday really your birthday?
surprised or checking sincerity of the birthday claim
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
あの人は学生じゃなかったんです。
That person wasn't actually a student.
correcting an assumption with an explanatory past reveal
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
本当に社員じゃなかったんですか？
Was he really not an employee?
disbelieving past negative status
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
明日、東京に行くんだ。
I'm going to Tokyo tomorrow (FYI).
casual announcement with explanatory feel; んだ without a specific audience
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
明日、どこか行くんだ？
Are you going somewhere tomorrow?
picking up on a cue and asking for the explanation in casual register
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
今日はバイトに行かないんだ。
I'm not going to work today (reason implied).
casual explanation for why you're free; reason implied
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
なんでバイトに行かないんだ？
Why aren't you going to work?
casual demand for explanation; slightly confrontational
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日、財布を落としたんだ。
I dropped my wallet yesterday (explaining).
casual disclosure of misfortune; sharing context informally
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日どこ行ったんだ？
Where did you go yesterday?
casual curious inquiry about past whereabouts
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
全然寝なかったんだ。
I didn't sleep at all (that's the story).
casual disclosure explaining visible tiredness or current alertness
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
なんで全然寝なかったんだ？
Why didn't you sleep at all?
demanding explanation for the sleepless night
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
この店のラーメン、本当に美味しいんだ。
The ramen at this place is seriously good.
casual emphatic endorsement; sharing a discovery with a friend
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
そんなに高いんだ？
Is it really that expensive?
casual surprised reaction to hearing a price
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
この映画、全然怖くないんだ。
This film isn't scary at all (honestly).
casual correction of expectation; reassuring someone it's not scary
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
本当に全然怖くないんだ？
Is it really not scary at all?
disbelieving the claim that it's not at all scary
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日の試合、すごく面白かったんだ。
Yesterday's match was incredibly interesting.
enthusiastic post-event report; sharing excitement with friends
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日の試合、そんなに面白かったんだ？
Was yesterday's match really that interesting?
reacting to someone's enthusiasm; seeking confirmation
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
そんなに難しくなかったんだ。
It wasn't that hard (actually).
casual reassurance after the fact
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
本当にそんなに難しくなかったんだ？
Was it really not that hard?
skeptical reaction to the reassurance
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あの先生、本当に親切なんだ。
That teacher is genuinely kind (let me tell you).
casual endorsement of someone's character with sincerity
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
あの先生、本当に親切なんだ？
Is that teacher really that kind?
checking or disbelieving the endorsement
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あそこ、そんなに便利じゃないんだ。
That place isn't that convenient (actually).
casual correction of someone's assumption about a place
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
本当にあそこ、そんなに便利じゃないんだ？
Is that place really not that convenient?
pushing back on the correction
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
子供の頃、あの公園が好きだったんだ。
I loved that park when I was a kid.
casual nostalgic disclosure; explaining a personal connection
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
子供の頃、本当にあそこが好きだったんだ？
Did you really love that place as a kid?
checking the authenticity of the nostalgic memory
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昔はそんなに料理が得意じゃなかったんだ。
I wasn't that good at cooking before.
casual self-disclosure about a past skill gap
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
昔、本当に料理が得意じゃなかったんだ？
Were you really not good at cooking before?
disbelief given the person's current evident skill
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
彼、実は歌手なんだ。
He's actually a singer (did you know?).
casual reveal with surprise element; 実は is the tell
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
彼、本当に歌手なんだ？
Is he really a singer?
processing the surprising information
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あれ、本物じゃないんだ。
That's not the real thing (I'm telling you).
casual revelation of inauthenticity
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
あれ、本当に本物じゃないんだ？
Is that really not the real thing?
seeking confirmation of that surprising revelation
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日は誕生日だったんだ。
Yesterday was my birthday (by the way).
casual explanation after the fact; 'oh, that's why...'
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日、誕生日だったんだ？
Was yesterday your birthday?
realizing the significance after having missed it
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あの人、実はプロじゃなかったんだ。
That person actually wasn't a pro.
casual revelation that someone wasn't what they appeared
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの人、本当にプロじゃなかったんだ？
Was that person really not a pro?
confirming the surprising downgrade in perceived status
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
Quick Recap
The copula family
だ · です · だろう · でしょう · んです
Comparison
Form Register Function Feels like
だ Plain State a fact — plain copula "Is / Am / Are"
です Polite State a fact — polite copula "Is / Am / Are"
だろう Casual Suppose or guess — no firm evidence "Probably / I bet"
でしょう Polite Suppose politely; soften a claim "Probably / I suppose"
んです / のです Polite Explain, give background, invite explanation "The fact is / You see / It's because"
When to use what
Stating a fact among friends and family → だ
Stating a fact in polite conversation → です
Guessing or supposing casually → だろう
Guessing politely, or softening a statement that would sound too direct → でしょう
Explaining yourself, giving the reason behind a situation, or showing interest in someone's reason → んです
Certainty scale: だ / です state, だろう / でしょう suppose — んです doesn't change certainty, it adds explanatory tone on top
N5
がある
(ga aru)
There is, Have — existence of inanimate things
Affirmative
Polite
Noun + があります
Casual
Noun + がある
Negative
Polite
Noun + がありません
Casual
Noun + がない
About

This grammar structure is used to express that something exists, is present, or is possessed — equivalent to "there is" or "to have" in English. ある is used exclusively for inanimate · non-living things: objects, events, places, time, abstract concepts. For living things, いる is used instead.

Location is expressed with the に particle before が: テーブルの上にケーキがある — "there is a cake on the table." In casual speech, が is often dropped entirely, leaving just the noun + ある.

ある is an う-verb, but its negative form is irregular: ない, not あらない. The polite negative is ありません. The kanji form 有る carries no difference in meaning and is common in written Japanese.

Examplesshow ▶
机の上に本があります。
There is a book on the desk.
basic existence statement for objects; に marks location, が marks the subject
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この辺に郵便局がありますか？
Is there a post office around here?
asking about nearby facilities; common practical question
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
冷蔵庫に牛乳がありません。
There is no milk in the fridge.
reporting absence of a specific item; common household exchange
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
この建物にエレベーターがありませんか？
Is there no elevator in this building?
slightly surprised or confirming absence of expected feature
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日、駅の近くに事故がありました。
There was an accident near the station yesterday.
reporting that an event occurred; 事故がある = 'there was an accident'
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日、会議がありましたか？
Was there a meeting yesterday?
confirming if a scheduled event took place
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
当時、この辺にコンビニがありませんでした。
There were no convenience stores around here back then.
historical absence; 'back then there were no...'
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
当時、この辺に学校がありませんでしたか？
Was there no school around here back then?
confirming past absence of a facility in an area
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
鞄の中に財布がある。
There is a wallet in the bag.
plain casual; checking own belongings or immediate surroundings
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
冷蔵庫にジュースある？
Is there any juice in the fridge?
most casual; が often dropped in informal speech
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
今日は時間がない。
I have no time today.
common complaint about being busy; 時間がない is a fixed phrase
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
財布に小銭ない？
Don't you have any coins in your wallet?
asking someone to check their change or small coins
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日、面白い授業があった。
There was an interesting class yesterday.
casual past; events and scheduled occurrences use ある
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日、宿題あった？
Was there homework yesterday?
casual school-life check on homework
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
子供の頃、この辺に公園がなかった。
There was no park around here when I was a child.
reflecting on what the neighborhood lacked in the past
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの頃、この辺にコンビニなかった？
Wasn't there a convenience store around here back then?
collaborative memory check; 'wasn't there one back then?'
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
がいる
(ga iru)
There is, Have — existence of living things
Affirmative
Polite
Noun + がいます
Casual
Noun + がいる
Negative
Polite
Noun + がいません
Casual
Noun + がいない
About

This grammar structure is used to express that a living thing exists, is present, or is possessed — equivalent to "there is" or "to have" in English. いる is used for living · animate things: people, animals, and by extension things operated or controlled by living beings (a police car with officers inside, for example). For inanimate objects and abstract things, ある is used instead.

Location is expressed with the に particle before が: 部屋に猫がいる — "there is a cat in the room." In casual speech, が is often dropped, leaving just the noun + いる.

いる is a る-verb (Ichidan verb): polite form います, negative いない, polite negative いません. The kanji form 居る is common in written Japanese and carries no difference in meaning.

Examplesshow ▶
公園に子供たちがいます。
There are children in the park.
basic animate existence; に location + が subject; いる for living things
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この学校に外国人の先生がいますか？
Are there any foreign teachers at this school?
asking about human presence at an institution
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今、オフィスに誰もいません。
There is no one in the office right now.
nobody present; 誰も + negative = no one at all
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
今、オフィスに誰もいませんか？
Is there no one in the office right now?
confirming or expressing mild surprise at expected emptiness
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日、図書館に山田さんがいました。
Yamada-san was at the library yesterday.
past presence of a specific person; common when recounting a sequence
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日、公園に子供たちがいましたか？
Were there children in the park yesterday?
asking about past presence at a location
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
授業中、教室に先生がいませんでした。
There was no teacher in the classroom during the lesson.
past absence of an expected person from a space
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
授業中、教室に先生がいませんでしたか？
Was there no teacher in the classroom during the lesson?
confirming or expressing surprise at an absence during a lesson
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
庭に猫がいる。
There is a cat in the garden.
casual plain; spotting an animal in a space you can observe
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
今、家に誰かいる？
Is anyone home right now?
very casual check on who's home; が often dropped
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
この辺に熊がいない。
There are no bears around here.
negative animate existence; stating an animal is absent from an area
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
この辺に猫いない？
Aren't there any cats around here?
casual; looking for animals; が dropped in speech
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
さっき、ここに田中さんがいた。
Tanaka-san was here just now.
noting someone was present just a moment ago
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
さっき、ここに田中さんいた？
Was Tanaka-san here just now?
casual past check; が dropped
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あの時、部屋に誰もいなかった。
There was no one in the room at that time.
past total absence; confirming no one was there at a specific moment
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの時、部屋に誰もいなかった？
Was there no one in the room at that time?
seeking confirmation of past emptiness
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
になる · くなる
(~ni naru · ~ku naru)
To become — change of state
Polite
い-adjective
い-adj [い] + く + なります
な-adjective
な-adjno な
+ になります
Noun
Noun + になります
Casual
い-adjective
い-adj [い] + く + なる
な-adjective
な-adjno な
+ になる
Noun
Noun + になる
About

This grammar structure is used to express a change of state — that something or someone is becoming, has become, or will become something. It applies to all three word types, each with a different connector before なる.

For い-adjectives, drop the final い and add く before なる: 寒い → 寒くなる. For な-adjectives, attach に directly to the stem — do not add な: 静か → 静かになる. For nouns, attach に directly: 先生 → 先生になる.

Exception: いい (good) does not follow the regular rule — it conjugates as よくなる, not いくなる.

なる conjugates normally for tense and polarity.

Examplesshow ▶
六月になると、日が長くなります。
When June comes, the days get longer.
natural cycle change; になる marks transition into a new state
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
朝はまだ明るくなりません。
The mornings still aren't getting lighter.
seasonal complaint about mornings not brightening yet
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
引っ越してから、部屋が少し広くなりました。
Since moving, the room has gotten a bit more spacious.
improvement after a life change; てから + なった = state changed after event
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先生が変わっても、宿題は難しくなりませんでした。
Even when the teacher changed, the homework didn't get harder.
expected change didn't materialize despite a trigger
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
毎日練習すれば、料理が上手になります。
If you practise every day, you'll get good at cooking.
conditional improvement; な-adj + になる for skill-based states
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
掃除しても、台所がきれいになりません。
Even after cleaning, the kitchen doesn't get clean.
frustration; effort not leading to the hoped-for clean state
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
薬を飲んで、弟が元気になりました。
My younger brother got better after taking medicine.
recovery from illness; cause + state change
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
何年経っても、姉はなかなか有名になりませんでした。
No matter how many years passed, my sister never managed to become famous.
persistent failure to achieve a goal despite years passing
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
来月、夏になります。
Next month it'll be summer.
season change; noun + になる
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ春になりません。
It still hasn't become spring.
mild impatience waiting for a season to arrive
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
去年、二人は友達になりました。
The two of them became friends last year.
relationship milestone; 友達になる is a fixed common phrase
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの人は結局、医者になりませんでした。
That person didn't end up becoming a doctor.
unrealized aspiration; 結局 = 'in the end' after a long wait
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
十二月になると、夜が短くなる。
When December comes, the nights get shorter.
seasonal pattern stated as general fact; plain casual
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
勉強しても、英語が易しくならない。
No matter how much I study, English doesn't get easier.
stubborn difficulty despite effort; expected improvement not coming
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
放っておいたら、お茶が冷たくなった。
I left it too long and the tea got cold.
unintended state change from neglect
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
夏なのに、全然暑くならなかった。
Even though it was summer, it didn't get hot at all.
unexpected absence of expected seasonal heat
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
最近、猫が好きになる。
I'm gradually coming to like cats.
gradual growing fondness; process-oriented use of なる
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
週末も仕事で、全然暇にならない。
I have work on weekends too, so I never get free time.
chronically unable to get free time; never reaching that state
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
新しい先生に変わってから、クラスが親切になった。
Since the new teacher took over, the class has become kinder.
class atmosphere improvement following a personnel change
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
弟はなかなか字が上手にならなかった。
My younger brother's handwriting never really got better.
slow progress despite effort; なかなか = 'just not quite getting there'
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
早く大人になる。
I want to grow up fast.
child's wish to grow up; plain volitional feel
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
来年はまだ高校生にならない。
I won't be a high school student until next year.
clarifying that a milestone won't be reached until next year
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
去年、姉がお母さんになった。
My older sister became a mother last year.
major life event stated plainly; noun + になった
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
今年は二十歳にならなかった。
I didn't turn 20 this year.
age milestone not yet reached in the stated year
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
III Verb Form Patterns
  1. 〜て〜て〜And then, After that — actions in sequence
  2. 〜ているIs · Am · Are ~ing — ongoing action
  3. 〜ているHas done, Is [result still present] — resulting state
  4. 〜ているKnows, Lives, Works — current state of being
  5. The three faces of ているrecap
  6. 〜たり〜たりするDo things like ~ and ~
  7. 〜たことがあるHave done before · Have the experience of doing
N5
〜て〜て〜
(~te · ~te · ~)
And then, After that — actions in sequence
Verb [て] te form / repeatable
、
Verb [て] te form / repeatable
、
Final Verb sets the tense
About

This grammar structure is used to list two or more actions happening in a fixed sequence — equivalent to "did X, then Y, then Z" in English. Each action in the chain is connected by the て-form (or で for verbs whose て-form ends in で), with a reading pause marked by 、between each.

Only the final verb carries the tense. All preceding て-forms are tense-neutral — they simply chain actions together. If the final verb is past (ました), the entire sequence is interpreted as past. If it is present or future (ます, dictionary form), the whole sequence refers to present or future.

This structure implies strict order — the actions happened one after another. It is not used for listing things that occurred in no particular order; for that, たり〜たりする is used instead.

Examplesshow ▶
毎朝、起きて、顔を洗って、朝ごはんを食べます。
Every morning I get up, wash my face, and eat breakfast.
sequential morning routine; て-form chains without changing the final tense
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
シャワーを浴びて、本を読んで、まだ寝ません。
I'll take a shower and read, but I won't sleep yet.
chain of winding-down activities with a surprising final negative
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
電車に乗って、音楽を聞いて、駅に着きました。
I got on the train, listened to music, and arrived at the station.
past sequential transit routine; events described in order
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
ご飯を作って、弟に電話して、食べませんでした。
I made food, called my younger brother, and didn't eat.
ironic chain; cooked and called but then didn't eat
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
家に帰って、シャワーを浴びて、寝る。
I'll come home, take a shower, and sleep.
end-of-day plain sequence; habitual routine
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
コーヒーを飲んで、音楽を聞いて、まだ寝ない。
I'll drink coffee and listen to music — I'm not sleeping yet.
casual night routine; not sleeping yet despite winding down
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
公園に行って、犬と遊んで、写真を撮った。
I went to the park, played with the dog, and took photos.
casual pleasant outing described step by step in past
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
財布を忘れて、何も買わなかった。
I forgot my wallet and didn't buy anything.
causal chain; forgetting the wallet meant no purchases
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
〜ている
(~te iru)
Is · Am · Are ~ing — ongoing action
Polite
Verb [て]te form
+ います
Casual
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
Very Casual — contraction
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
About

This grammar structure is used to express an action that is currently in progress. It is the closest equivalent to the English present continuous ("I am doing"), though it is not a perfect match. It is formed by attaching いる to the て-form of any verb; verbs whose て-form ends in で follow the same pattern.

The final meaning depends on the verb, but in this group the action is actively ongoing — something is happening right now and has not stopped.

In casual speech, いる contracts to る — dropping the い entirely. This gives forms like 食べてる, 見てる, 読んでる. The contraction is extremely common in everyday spoken Japanese.

Examplesshow ▶
父は台所で料理しています。
My father is cooking in the kitchen.
present ongoing action; で marks the location of the activity
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今、何をしていますか？
What are you doing right now?
standard 'what are you doing?' in polite register
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
弟はまだ勉強していません。
My younger brother is still not studying.
negative progressive; まだ implies they should have started by now
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
まだ準備していませんか？
Haven't you prepared yet?
polite prompt checking if preparation has begun
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日の夜、雨が降っていました。
It was raining last night.
past ongoing background event; describes weather during another activity
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日の夜、何をしていましたか？
What were you doing last night?
past 'what were you doing?' used to establish context
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
そのとき、先生はまだ話していませんでした。
The teacher was still not speaking at that point.
past negative progressive; hadn't started talking at that point
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
そのとき、まだ食べていませんでしたか？
Were you still not eating at that point?
checking if something hadn't yet begun at a reference moment
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
兄は今、本を読んでいる。
My older brother is reading a book right now.
casual present progressive; matter-of-fact observation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
今、何してる？
What are you doing right now?
most casual version; ている contracts to てる in speech
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
今は音楽を聞いていない。
I'm not listening to music right now.
casual negative progressive; not doing it at this moment
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
まだ準備してない？
Aren't you ready yet?
casual check; contraction of していない
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
公園に着いたとき、子供たちが走っていた。
When I arrived at the park, the children were running.
backgrounded past action; describes what was happening when speaker arrived
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日の夜、何してた？
What were you doing last night?
very casual past inquiry; past of してる
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
そのとき、犬はまだ眠っていなかった。
The dog was still not sleeping at that point.
past negative progressive; dog hadn't yet fallen asleep
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
その時、まだ寝てなかった？
Were you still not asleep at that point?
casual check on past wakefulness
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
〜ている
(~te iru)
Has done, Is [result still present] — resulting state
Polite
Verb [て]te form
+ います
Casual
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
Very Casual — contraction
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
About

This grammar structure is used to express a resulting state — an action has already been completed, but its effect or condition is still present. The focus is not on the action itself, but on the fact that it happened and the result still holds. Think of it as "has done, and it is still that way."

This use is dominated by intransitive verbs — verbs that describe a state change without a direct object. The subject enters a new state, and ている describes that state as currently true. The action occurred; what you are describing is the aftermath.

The key to recognizing this use is the verb type. Intransitive verbs describing a state change — opening, closing, stopping, falling — almost always signal resulting state rather than ongoing action.

Examplesshow ▶
窓が開いています。
The window is open.
resultant state; someone opened it and it remains open — key distinction from 開けている
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ店は開いていますか？
Is the shop still open?
checking if a place is currently in an open state; very common practical question
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
電気がまだ消えていません。
The light is still not off.
light still on despite expectation it would be off; resultant state negated
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
まだ問題が解決していませんか？
Hasn't the problem been resolved yet?
checking if an unresolved issue persists; 解決していない = problem remains
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
朝から、ドアが閉まっていました。
The door had been closed since morning.
past resultant state; door had been in closed state since morning
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
朝から電気がついていましたか？
Was the light on since morning?
asking about past resultant state of a light being on
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
修理の前、電気がついていませんでした。
The light was not on before the repair.
pre-repair state; light was not in the on state
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日の朝、窓がまだ閉まっていませんでしたか？
Was the window still not closed yesterday morning?
past negative resultant state; window hadn't been put into the shut state yet
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
道に車が止まっている。
A car is parked on the road.
casual observation of a parked car; resultant state of parking
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
ドア、まだ開いてる？
Is the door still open?
casual check; contraction of 開いている
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
財布はまだ落ちていない。
The wallet hasn't fallen yet.
wallet hasn't reached a fallen state yet; まだ + resultant state
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
洗濯物、まだ乾いてない？
Isn't the laundry dry yet?
checking if laundry has reached a dry state
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
家に帰ったとき、弟は疲れていた。
When I got home, my younger brother was tired.
past resultant state of a person; tired = in a state of fatigue
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
家に着いた時、電気ついてた？
Was the light on when you got home?
casual past check on whether the light was in the on state
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日は試合がまだ終わっていなかった。
The match was still not over yesterday.
past negative resultant state; game was still ongoing
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
試合が終わった時、まだスコアが決まってなかった？
Was the score still not decided when the match ended?
past state of an undecided score at a moment when game ended
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
〜ている
(~te iru)
Knows, Lives, Works — current state of being
Polite
Verb [て]te form
+ います
Casual
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
Very Casual — contraction
Verb [て]te form
+ いる
About

This grammar structure is used to express a current condition, status, or state of being — something that is simply true right now, with no active process or completed action behind it. The subject just is that way.

This use applies to a fixed set of stative and mental verbs — verbs like "to know," "to understand," "to live," "to work," "to own," "to be married." These verbs do not describe physical activity or change; they describe how things stand. The set of verbs that carry this meaning is largely fixed — you cannot apply it freely to new verbs the way you can with ongoing action. Almost all verbs in this group are う-verbs.

Examplesshow ▶
父の電話番号を知っています。
I know my father's phone number.
知る used in stative te-form; 知っています = know (not 'currently knowing')
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女の住所を知っていますか？
Do you know her address?
asking about current known information
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
漢字の意味が分かっていません。
I don't understand the meaning of the kanji.
not understanding; 分かる in stative te-form
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
まだ結果が分かっていませんか？
Don't you know the result yet?
result still unknown; まだ + stative negative
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
その頃、大阪に住んでいました。
I was living in Osaka back then.
continuous residence at a past time period; common for life-stage descriptions
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
当時、東京に住んでいましたか？
Were you living in Tokyo back then?
past habitual residence question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
去年まで、この会社で働いていませんでした。
I wasn't working at this company until last year.
stative negation of employment up to a certain point
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
去年は東京で働いていませんでしたか？
Weren't you working in Tokyo last year?
confirming past workplace status
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
姉はかわいい犬を二匹持っている。
My older sister has two cute dogs.
stative ownership; 持つ+ている = have/own something
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
田中さんのこと、知ってる？
Do you know Tanaka-san?
casual contraction; asking if someone knows Tanaka as a person
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
あいつ、結婚していない。
That guy isn't married.
stative unmarried status; plain casual
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
まだ答えが分かってない？
Don't you know the answer yet?
casual stative negative; still don't know the answer
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昔、あいつは私の姉と付き合っていた。
That guy used to date my older sister.
past relationship state; 付き合う+ている = be dating
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昔、大阪に住んでた？
Were you living in Osaka before?
casual past residence question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昔、あの兄弟は全然似ていなかった。
Those siblings didn't look alike at all before.
past stative comparison; 似る+ていない = don't resemble each other
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
子供の頃、眼鏡かけてなかった？
Weren't you wearing glasses as a child?
past accessory or habit state question; casual
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
Quick Recap
The three faces of ている
ongoing action · resulting state · current state of being
Comparison
Use Typical verbs Function Feels like
Ongoing action 食べる / 読む / 走る — durative action verbs An action in progress at this very moment "Is / Am / Are ~ing"
Resulting state 結婚する / 死ぬ / 開く — change-of-state verbs A change happened earlier and its result still holds now "Has done" / "Is [result still present]"
State of being 住む / 働く / 知る — verbs of life circumstance An ongoing condition of someone's life, not an event "Knows / Lives / Works"
When to use what
The action is unfolding right now, in the middle of happening → 食べている — is eating
Something changed at some point and the result is still true now → 結婚している — is married
A long-term fact about someone — residence, occupation, knowledge → 住んでいる — lives
Same form, three readings — the verb's meaning decides: durative verbs read as ongoing, change-of-state verbs read as result, life-circumstance verbs read as state
N5
〜たり〜たりする
(~tari ~tari suru)
Do things like ~ and ~
representative actions — no fixed order
Verb A [た] past form
+ り +
Verb B [た] past form
+ り + する
single action — implies "among other things"
Verb [た] past form
+ り + する
About

This grammar structure is used to list representative actions — things you did or do, without implying that the list is complete or that the actions happened in a specific order. The closest English equivalent is "do things like A and B."

Each verb is put into its plain past (た-form) with り added after it. Only the final する is conjugated for tense and polarity — します for present polite, しました for past polite, しません for negative.

〜たり vs 〜て: the て-form connects actions in a strict sequence. 〜たり makes no claim about order and signals the list is not exhaustive. Use て when sequence matters; use たり when giving examples.

Single verb use: たりする can follow a single verb, implying the speaker did that action among other unspecified things.

Note: in sequences where する itself is the last listed verb, する at the end is often omitted in natural speech.

Examplesshow ▶
休みの日は料理したり、散歩したりします。
On days off I do things like cook and go for walks.
listing non-exhaustive activities; implies other things also happen on days off
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
最近、外に出たり、友達と話したりしません。
Lately I don't do things like go out or talk with friends.
negative たりform; listing specific things that are notably absent from routine
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
子供の頃、木に登ったり、川で泳いだりしました。
When I was a kid I did things like climb trees and swim in the river.
past childhood activities; typical nostalgic phrasing with open-ended feel
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
旅行中、買い物したり、写真を撮ったりしませんでした。
During the trip I didn't do things like go shopping or take photos.
past trip activities not done; implying a disappointing or quiet trip
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
夜は本を読んだり、テレビを見たりする。
At night I do things like read and watch TV.
casual evening routine; the たりform conveys 'among other things'
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
仕事が忙しくて、運動したり、ゆっくり寝たりしない。
Work is so busy I don't do things like exercise or sleep properly.
causal chain; busyness preventing normal life activities
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
昨日は友達とゲームをしたり、ご飯を食べたりした。
Yesterday I did things like play games and eat with friends.
casual past social time described as an open-ended pair of activities
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
学生の頃、あまり勉強したり、本を読んだりしなかった。
When I was a student I didn't really do things like study or read books.
admitting to not being academically diligent in student days
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
〜たことがある
(~ta koto ga aru)
Have done before · Have the experience of doing
affirmative — have done before
Verb [た] past form
+ ことがある
negative — have never done
Verb [た] past form
+ ことがない / ことがありません
About

This grammar structure is used to express that you have done something at least once — that there has been a time in your life when X happened. It translates as "I have done X before" or "I have the experience of doing X." The focus is on whether the experience exists, not on when it happened.

Only the verb goes into the plain past (た-form). ある carries all tense and polarity. こと nominalizes the verb phrase. の cannot replace こと in this expression.

Questions and responses: ことがありますか turns the structure into "Have you ever...?" The natural responses are はい、あります and いいえ、ありません.

Examplesshow ▶
京都に行ったことがあります。
I have been to Kyoto.
classic experience statement; the event is in the past and stored as knowledge
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
富士山に登ったことがありますか？
Have you ever climbed Mount Fuji?
asking about a lifetime experience; very common travel conversation topic
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
お酒を飲んだことがありません。
I have never drunk alcohol.
total absence of a specific experience; often used for food or travel
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
海外に住んだことがありませんか？
Have you never lived abroad?
slightly surprised or curious that someone has never lived abroad
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
子供の頃、あの山に登ったことがありました。
I had climbed that mountain when I was a child.
past perfect; had the experience before a reference point in time
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
以前、この店に来たことがありましたか？
Had you ever been to this shop before?
asking about prior experience at a specific place
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
その頃はまだ日本語を勉強したことがありませんでした。
I had never studied Japanese at that point.
no prior study experience at a particular time in the past
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
その頃、まだ一人で旅行したことがありませんでしたか？
Hadn't you ever travelled alone at that point?
asking if someone had ever traveled solo up to a certain point
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
一人で飛行機に乗ったことがある。
I have been on a plane by myself.
plain casual milestone experience; flying alone for the first time
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
本物の寿司、食べたことある？
Have you ever eaten real sushi?
casual food experience question; very common between friends
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
生の魚を食べたことがない。
I have never eaten raw fish.
plain negative; total lifetime non-experience; common for foreigners
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
一度も負けたことない？
Have you really never lost even once?
一度も + negative = 'not even once'; rhetorical check on a perfect record
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昔、あの映画を見たことがあった。
I had seen that film before.
past perfect casual; recalls having had the experience before a reference time
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
子供の頃、外国に行ったことあった？
Had you ever been abroad as a child?
casual past perfect query about childhood travel
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
その頃、海で泳いだことがなかった。
I had never swum in the sea at that point.
had never done it up to that point in the past
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
あの頃、まだ誰かに告白したことなかった？
Hadn't you ever confessed your feelings to anyone back then?
checking romantic inexperience up to a specific age; slightly intimate question
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
IV Demonstratives & Question Words
  1. こそあどDemonstrative reference system
  2. Question WordsOpen-ended question words
  3. なぜ · どうして · なんでWhy — formal → neutral → casual
  4. WH-word + か · もSomeone · Somewhere · No one · Nowhere
  5. Noun の [location word]Relative position expressions
N5
こそあど
(ko-so-a-do)
Demonstrative reference system
こ
near speaker
そ
near listener
あ
far from both
ど
question
Reference
Type こ そ あ ど
Thing (pronoun) これ それ あれ どれ
Modifier (pre-noun) この その あの どの
Place ここ そこ あそこ どこ
About

This grammar structure is used to point to things, modify nouns, and refer to places relative to where the speaker and listener are. The four stems — こ, そ, あ, ど — represent distance: near the speaker, near the listener, far from both, and unknown (question).

Critical rule — standalone vs noun-required: この · その · あの · どの must always be followed directly by a noun.
