They cannot stand alone under any circumstances, not even in casual speech. これ · それ · あれ · どれ and ここ · そこ · あそこ · どこ stand alone as complete words. If you want to say "this book," use この本. If you want to say "this one" without naming the noun, use これ.

これ · それ · あれ · どれ — thing pronouns: These refer to things and ideas, not people. For people use この人 · あの人 etc. どれ is used when choosing from three or more options — for two options, どちら is used instead. In casual speech these can appear alone if context is clear (これ？、それ。), but in full sentences they need a verb, です, or a particle.

この · その · あの · どの — noun modifiers: These work for both objects and people. あの人 (that person over there) is the standard way to refer to someone at a distance.

この and これ serve different grammatical roles and cannot replace each other. この modifies a noun that follows it; これ stands in place of the noun entirely. Mixing them up is one of the most common early mistakes.

ここ · そこ · あそこ · どこ — place words: These refer only to places — never to things or people. They function like location nouns and combine with place particles depending on purpose:

〜にある · いる existence at a location — where something/someone is
〜に行く · 来る arrival at a location — used with motion verbs
〜へ行く · 来る direction toward a location — emphasis on movement
〜で〜する place where an action occurs
Examplesshow ▶
これは私のかばんです。
This is my bag.
これ for items near the speaker; basic possession identification
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
それは姉のコートです。
That (near you) is my sister's coat.
それ for items near the listener; used when the thing is with the other person
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あれは有名な神社です。
That over there is a famous shrine.
あれ for items far from both; requires shared visual reference
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
どれが田中さんのですか？
Which one is Tanaka-san's?
どれ asks 'which one' when selecting from multiple visible options
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この本、面白いよ。
This book is interesting.
この directly modifies a nearby noun; item in speaker's vicinity
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
その映画はもう見ました。
I've already seen that film.
その references something the listener mentioned or brought up
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの先生は授業がわかりやすい。
That teacher's lessons are easy to understand.
あの for a mutually known referent; 'that teacher we both know'
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
どの電車に乗りますか？
Which train do you take?
どの + noun asks 'which specific one' from a known set
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
ここは静かでいい場所です。
This is a quiet, nice place.
ここ for location near the speaker
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
鍵はそこに置いてください。
Please put the key there.
そこ near the listener; directing someone to a specific spot near them
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あそこに郵便局があります。
There's a post office over there.
あそこ for a far location visible to both parties
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
トイレはどこですか？
Where is the bathroom?
どこ asks for an unknown location
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
N5
Question Words
(interrogatives)
Open-ended question words
Question word + normal sentence word order + か
Reference
Japanese Meaning Notes
どこ where for locations — see こそあど card
いつ when for time; に optional
だれ who for people; どなた is the polite form
なに · なん what becomes なん before certain sounds — see below
どれ which one? standalone; 3+ options — see こそあど card
どの which (noun)? always followed by a noun — see こそあど card
いくら how much? used for price · cost
なんじ what time? compound: なに + じ (time)
なんようび what day of the week? compound: なに + ようび
なに vs なん — pronunciation shift

Use なん instead of なに when the following word begins with a d-line sound (だ、で、ど) or an n-line sound (な、に、ぬ、ね、の). This is purely for ease of pronunciation — both mean "what." Examples: なんですか、なんじ、なんようび、なんにん。

Particle compatibility
Question word Common particles Function
どこ に、で、から location of action · movement
いつ に (optional) time of action
だれ が、を、に subject, object, target
なに · なん を、が、に object or subject of sentence
どれ が choosing among 3+ standalone items
どの noun + が · を choosing with attached noun
About

Question words follow normal Japanese word order — the rest of the sentence stays the same. What marks a sentence as a question is the final particle か in polite speech, or rising intonation in casual speech where か is dropped.

なぜ · どうして · なんで (why) are covered separately in their own card.

Examplesshow ▶
だれがこのケーキを食べましたか？
Who ate this cake?
誰 with が marks who performed the action; subject question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
机の上のなにを探しているの？
What are you looking for on the desk?
何 + を asks what object is being searched for
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
それはなんですか？
What is that?
なん before ですか; asking for identity
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
駅はどこですか？
Where is the station?
どこ always for locations
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
試験はいつですか？
When is the exam?
いつ always for time reference
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
このコートはいくらですか？
How much is this coat?
いくら always for prices
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
りんご、いくつ買った？
How many apples did you buy?
いくつ for countable items without a specific counter; casual register
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
新しい仕事はどうですか？
How is the new job?
どう asks for an overall impression or how something is going
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
どんな音楽が好きですか？
What kind of music do you like?
どんな + noun asks 'what kind of'; invites a descriptive answer
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今、なんじですか？
What time is it now?
なんじ = what time; なん is used before time-related counters
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日はなんようびですか？
What day of the week is it today?
なんようび = what day of the week; common classroom or scheduling question
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
N5
なぜ · どうして · なんで
(naze · doushite · nande)
Why — formal → neutral → casual
formal
なぜ + Question
casual
なんで + Question
neutral
どうして + Question
About

All three words ask "why" and are structurally identical — they sit at the start of a question or embed mid-sentence. The only meaningful difference is register.

なぜ is the most formal. It is common in writing, formal speeches, news, and serious situations where a logical reason or cause is being demanded. In everyday casual conversation it can sound stiff or even confrontational.

どうして is the neutral default. It works in both polite and casual speech without sounding odd in either. Literally it breaks down as どう (how) + して (te-form of する) — "how did it come to be that way?" — so it can carry a nuance of curiosity or mild surprise rather than a hard demand for logic. When in doubt, どうして is the safest choice.

なんで is the most casual. It is frequent in everyday conversation with friends, family, and close acquaintances. It can come across as direct, demanding, or even accusatory depending on tone, so it is best avoided with strangers or people of higher status. Literally it is 何 (what) + で (particle for means) — "by what means" — which is why in some contexts なんで can also mean "how." The clearer form for that meaning is なにで.

One word to keep separate: どうした looks similar to どうして but means "what's wrong?" or "what happened?" — it expresses concern, not a why-question. It is often followed by の in casual speech (どうしたの？).

Examplesshow ▶
なぜ毎日学校に来るんですか？
Why do you come to school every day?
formal register; なぜ sounds written or academic in casual conversation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
なぜこの部屋はこんなに暗いんですか？
Why is this room so dark?
formal question about a state; feels investigative or official
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
なぜ昨日は部屋が静かだったんですか？
Why was the room quiet yesterday?
formal past inquiry into why a situation occurred
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
なぜあの人は先生じゃなかったんですか？
Why wasn't that person a teacher?
formal question about a surprising past negative status
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
どうして学校に来なかったの？
Why didn't you come to school?
conversational register; neutral and softer than なぜ
VERB PAST NEGATIVE NEUTRAL QUESTION
どうして昨日のテストはそんなに難しかったの？
Why was yesterday's test so hard?
seeking explanation after the fact; common student or peer register
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE NEUTRAL QUESTION
どうしてそんなに元気じゃないの？
Why aren't you feeling well?
caring inquiry into wellbeing; どうして fits better than なぜ here
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE NEUTRAL QUESTION
どうして今日は休みなんですか？
Why is today a day off?
neutral-to-polite why; seeking an institutional or practical explanation
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE NEUTRAL QUESTION
なんで泣いてるの？
Why are you crying?
most casual register; なんで = 何で; used between close friends only
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
なんでこのご飯はこんなに辛いの？
Why is this food so spicy?
casual complaint framed as a question
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
なんで昨日は元気じゃなかったの？
Why weren't you feeling well yesterday?
casual concerned inquiry about past low energy
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
なんであいつが先生だったの？
Why was that guy the teacher?
casual disbelief or gossip; slightly negative undertone
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
顔色が悪いけど、どうしたの？
You look pale — what's wrong?
どうした = 'what happened?' rather than 'why'; shows direct concern for the person
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
N5
WH-word + か · も
(~ka · ~mo)
Someone · Somewhere · No one · Nowhere
positive
WH-word + か + affirmative sentence
negative
WH-word + も +
Verbnegative form
Reference
Base word + か (positive) + も (negative)
だれ だれか — someone · anyone だれも — no one
どこ どこか — somewhere · anywhere どこも — nowhere
なに なにか — something · anything なにも — nothing
About

This grammar structure is used to turn question words into indefinite pronouns. Adding か to a question word makes it mean "some-" or "any-" in positive sentences. Adding も paired with a negative verb makes it mean "no-" or "not any-."

English "anyone" covers both uses — Japanese keeps them separate: だれか for the positive ("is anyone here?") and だれも〜ない for the negative ("no one came"). Mixing them up is a common mistake.

When another particle is also present — such as に or へ — か slots in before the particle and も slots in after it. The verb must be negative when using も.

だれかに話した Talked to someone — か before に
どこかへ行った Went somewhere — か before へ
だれかと食べた Ate with someone — か before と
どこかで見た Saw it somewhere — か before で
だれにも話さなかった Didn't talk to anyone — も after に
どこへも行かなかった Didn't go anywhere — も after へ
だれとも食べなかった Didn't eat with anyone — も after と
どこでも見なかった Didn't see it anywhere — も after で
Examplesshow ▶
だれかが窓を叩いています。
Someone is knocking on the window.
だれか = someone unknown; common in describing sounds or events
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昨日、だれかが電話してきました。
Someone called yesterday.
someone called but identity unknown; typical in daily speech
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
だれかに聞いてみる。
I'll ask someone.
planning to consult an unspecified person; casual forward-looking
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
財布の中にだれかの写真があった。
There was someone's photo in my wallet.
mysterious discovery; someone's photo with no known owner
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
教室にだれもいません。
There is no one in the classroom.
だれも + negative = no one at all; total absence of people
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
パーティーにだれも来ませんでした。
No one came to the party.
no one showed up; universal non-attendance
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
そのニュース、だれも知らない。
Nobody knows about that news.
universally unknown piece of news; niche or obscure information
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
試合でだれも点を入れなかった。
Nobody scored in the match.
scoreless match; sports context
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
週末にどこかへ行きますか？
Are you going somewhere this weekend?
asking about unspecified plans to go somewhere this weekend
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日、どこかで財布を落としました。
I dropped my wallet somewhere yesterday.
unknown location loss; reporting a lost item
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
どこかで休もう。
Let's rest somewhere.
casual suggestion; specific place to be determined later
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
昨日どこかに鍵を置いた。
I put my key somewhere yesterday.
vague memory of where something was left
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
今日はどこも開いていません。
Nowhere is open today.
どこも + negative = nowhere is open; holiday or shutdown context
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
旅行中、どこへも行きませんでした。
During the trip, I didn't go anywhere.
didn't go anywhere during a trip; stayed in one place
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
疲れてどこにも行きたくない。
I'm tired and don't want to go anywhere.
too tired to want to go anywhere; どこにも + negative
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
昨日はどこにも出かけなかった。
Yesterday I didn't go out anywhere.
total stay-at-home day; no outings at all
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
飲み物はなにかありますか？
Is there something to drink?
asking if any drinks are available; vague and polite
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日、なにか食べましたか？
Did you eat something yesterday?
checking if any food was consumed at all
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
なにか面白いことない？
Is there anything interesting going on?
casual; looking for entertainment or gossip
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
昨日、なにか買った？
Did you buy something yesterday?
casual post-outing inquiry about purchases
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL QUESTION
バッグにはなにも入っていません。
There's nothing in the bag.
nothing at all inside; emphatic emptiness
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
今朝はなにも飲みませんでした。
I didn't drink anything this morning.
drank nothing; fasting morning or rushed start
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
お腹が痛くてなにも食べたくない。
My stomach hurts and I don't want to eat anything.
stomach pain removing any desire to eat
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
昨日はなにもしなかった。
I didn't do anything yesterday.
completely inactive day; common lazy-day expression
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
N5
Noun の [location word]
(Noun no [location])
Relative position expressions
action
Noun + の + location word + で + Verb
location
Noun + の + location word + です
existence
Noun + の + location word + に + ある / いる
Reference
Kanji Hiragana Meaning
右 みぎ right
左 ひだり left
前 まえ in front of
後ろ うしろ behind
中 なか inside
上 うえ on · above
下 した under · beneath
近く ちかく near
隣 となり next to · beside
間 あいだ between — requires X と Y のあいだ
About

This grammar structure is used to describe where something is positioned relative to another object. Unlike ここ · そこ · あそこ which are complete location words on their own, these words are relative — they cannot stand alone. They always require a reference noun connected by の: テーブルの下 (under the table), 駅の前 (in front of the station).

The particle after the location phrase depends on what follows. Use です to identify where something is. Use に with ある or いる to say something exists at that location. Use で to mark where an action takes place.

あいだ (between) requires two reference nouns joined by と: デパートと病院のあいだ (between the department store and the hospital).

Examplesshow ▶
エレベーターは角を曲がって右です。
The elevator is to the right after the corner.
giving directions; right after a corner
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昔、駅の右に大きな木があった。
There used to be a big tree to the right of the station.
describing past landscape to orient a historical scene
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
入り口の左に先生がいます。
There is a teacher to the left of the entrance.
positional statement with animate subject; いる + に for location
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
出口の左に何もない。
There's nothing to the left of the exit.
nothing to the left; direction + nothing construction
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
駅の前で友達を待ちました。
I waited for my friend in front of the station.
common meeting spot description; 前 of a known landmark
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
家の前に猫がいる。
There's a cat in front of the house.
casual observation of an animal's position
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
椅子は机の後ろです。
The chair is behind the desk.
behind; directional noun used as location descriptor
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
映画館の後ろに誰もいなかった。
There was no one behind the cinema.
scene description establishing that no one was behind a place
VERB PAST NEGATIVE CASUAL
箱の中に手紙があります。
There is a letter inside the box.
inside a container; 中 + ある for contained objects
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
図書館の中で勉強した。
I studied inside the library.
で marks activity location, not destination
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
木の上に鳥がいます。
There is a bird on the tree.
classic animate existence sentence; bird perched above
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
机の上に何もない。
There's nothing on the desk.
nothing on top; casual plain observation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
机の下で犬が寝ていました。
The dog was sleeping under the desk.
under the desk; past ongoing state
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
椅子の下に靴がある。
There are shoes under the chair.
shoes under a chair; plain casual spatial observation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
駅の近くに公園があります。
There is a park near the station.
proximity expression; near a known landmark
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
学校の近くで友達に会った。
I met a friend near the school.
casual past; meeting someone in the vicinity of a place
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
花屋は駅の隣です。
The flower shop is next to the station.
adjacent location; 隣 = directly next to
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
隣の席に誰もいない。
There's no one in the seat next to me.
seat next to the speaker is empty; relatable commuting situation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE CASUAL
バス停は銀行と本屋の間です。
The bus stop is between the bank and the bookstore.
between two known landmarks; 間 = between
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昔、山と海の間に小さな町があった。
There used to be a small town between the mountains and the sea.
past geographical description of a place between natural features
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE CASUAL
V Preferences, Ability & Desire
  1. が好き · がきらいLike · Dislike — expressing preference
  2. のが上手 · のが下手Good at · Bad at — expressing skill level
  3. 〜たいWant to do ~
N5
が好き · がきらい
(~ga suki · ~ga kirai)
Like · Dislike — expressing preference
Polite
like
Noun + が好きです
dislike
Noun + がきらいです
Casual
like
Noun + が好きだ
dislike
Noun + がきらいだ
About

This grammar structure is used to express likes and dislikes toward nouns — objects, people, or abstract things. 好き means "like" and きらい means "dislike." Prefixing either with 大 amplifies the intensity: 大好き means "love" and 大嫌い means "hate."

Despite translating like verbs in English, all four are な-adjectives — they describe an emotional state, not an action. Note that きらい ends in い but is still a な-adjective, not an い-adjective. It conjugates exactly like 好き.

The thing you like or dislike is marked with が, not を. This is because が marks it as the subject of the feeling — the thing that triggers the emotion — rather than a direct object being acted upon.

Negation follows standard な-adjective rules: 好きじゃない · 好きじゃないです (don't like), きらいじゃない (don't dislike — roughly "fine with it").

Examplesshow ▶
子供の頃から、音楽が好きです。
I've liked music since I was a child.
lifelong preference; から marks the starting point of the feeling
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
日本料理が好きですか？
Do you like Japanese food?
simple polite preference question; 好き takes が not を
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
人混みが好きじゃありません。
I don't like crowds.
polite negative; じゃありません is more formal than じゃないです
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
辛い食べ物がきらいじゃありませんか？
Don't you dislike spicy food?
unexpected question assuming dislike; slight surprise in the phrasing
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
学生の頃、数学が好きでした。
I liked maths when I was a student.
past academic preference; 好き in past tense with でした
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
子供の頃、どんな食べ物が好きでしたか？
What food did you like when you were a child?
asking about childhood food preferences
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
子供の頃、野菜が好きじゃありませんでした。
I didn't like vegetables as a child.
polite past negative; very common structure when discussing childhood dislikes
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昔、本当に勉強がきらいじゃありませんでしたか？
Didn't you really dislike studying before?
double-checking past dislike; slightly ironic
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼女は静かな場所が好きだ。
She likes quiet places.
plain assertion about a third party's preference
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
お前、本当にホラー映画が好きだ？
Do you really like horror films?
casual male speech; お前 is direct; surprised questioning
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
早起きがきらいじゃない、ただ苦手なだけだ。
It's not that I dislike waking up early — I'm just not good at it.
nuance: きらい (dislike) vs 苦手 (not comfortable with / not good at) — different feelings
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
あいつ、本当に会議がきらいじゃない？
Does he really not dislike meetings?
casual questioning of a claim; disbelief at someone's patience
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
昔、あの監督の映画が大好きだった。
I used to love that director's films.
大好き = love (stronger than 好き); plain past
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃、どんなスポーツが好きだった？
What sports did you like as a child?
casual past preference question
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
高校のとき、英語がきらいじゃなかった。むしろ得意だった。
I didn't dislike English in high school. I was actually good at it.
むしろ = 'rather / actually'; used to correct and flip the expectation
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
昔、本当に数学がきらいじゃなかった？
Did you really not dislike maths before?
casual check on past attitude toward a subject
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
のが上手 · のが下手
(~no ga jouzu · ~no ga heta)
Good at · Bad at — expressing skill level
Polite
good at
Verb dictionary form
+ のが上手です
bad at
Verb dictionary form
+ のが下手です
Casual
good at
Verb dictionary form
+ のが上手だ
bad at
Verb dictionary form
+ のが下手だ
About

This grammar structure is used to express how skilled or unskilled someone is at performing an action. 上手 means "good at" and 下手 means "bad at." Both are な-adjectives and conjugate normally.

の nominalizes the verb phrase — it turns the action into a noun-like concept that can be the subject of the predicate. が then marks that noun as the subject of the feeling of skill or lack of it. The verb always stays in the dictionary form, regardless of tense. Tense is expressed by conjugating 上手 or 下手 instead.

One social note: saying 上手ですね directly to someone about their own ability can come across as condescending — as if you're surprised or judging from above. It is safer when talking about a third person or when asked for an opinion.

Examplesshow ▶
田中先生は文法を説明するのが上手です。
Tanaka-sensei is good at explaining grammar.
の nominalizes the verb clause; 上手 and 下手 always take が
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女は料理を作るのが上手です。
She is good at cooking.
standard pattern; cooking as a nominalized activity
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あなたは人前で話すのが上手ですか？
Are you good at speaking in front of people?
asking about public speaking ability
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼は人の名前を覚えるのが下手だ。
He is bad at remembering people's names.
plain; naming a specific weakness around memory
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
弟は時間を守るのが下手だ。
My younger brother is bad at keeping to time.
punctuality described as a persistent weakness
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
お前、絵を描くのが上手だ？
Are you good at drawing?
casual masculine question; slightly surprised tone
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
山田さんは若い頃、絵を描くのが上手でした。
Yamada-san was good at drawing when she was young.
past skill with でした; used to describe a former talent
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
父は若い頃、人前で話すのが下手でした。
My father was bad at speaking in front of people when young.
past weakness; implying improvement since then
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
子供の頃、泳ぐのが上手でしたか？
Were you good at swimming as a child?
asking about childhood swimming ability
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
子供の頃、泳ぐのが下手だったけど、今は得意だ。
I was bad at swimming as a child, but I'm good at it now.
before-and-after skill comparison; now/then contrast
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
学生の頃、数学を解くのが下手だった。
I was bad at solving maths problems when I was a student.
past academic weakness; describing student struggles
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昔、料理するのが上手だった？
Were you good at cooking before?
casual past skill question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
〜たい
(~tai)
Want to do ~
Polite
want
Verb stem + たいです
don’t want
Verb stem + たくないです
Casual
want
Verb stem + たい
don’t want
Verb stem + たくない
About

This grammar structure is used to express a desire or aspiration to perform an action — equivalent to "want to do ~" in English. たい attaches to the verb stem (the same base that ます attaches to).

Tense and polarity: たい conjugates exactly like a い-adjective — four forms: たい (present affirmative) → たくない (negative) → たかった (past) → たくなかった (past negative). Adding です to any form makes it polite.

を vs が: verbs that normally take を can also take が when used with たい. を is neutral and marks the action toward the object. が shifts focus onto the object of desire itself, adding an emotional or selective nuance. Both are grammatically correct — を is the safer default.

First-person restriction: たい is for expressing your own desires or directly asking someone about theirs. It cannot be used to guess or assume what a third party wants — for that, たがっている is used instead.

Not for requests or invitations: たい cannot be used to tell someone you want them to do something (use てほしい) or to invite someone to do something together (use ませんか).

Examplesshow ▶
田中さんは今年中に日本語を上達させたいです。
Tanaka-san wants to improve her Japanese by the end of the year.
third-person たい; works when the internal state is known to the speaker
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
弟はいつかプロのサッカー選手になりたいです。
My younger brother wants to become a professional footballer someday.
aspiration; いつか = someday; typical for long-term dreams
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
将来、何になりたいですか？
What do you want to become in the future?
classic question about future career or life aspirations
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼女は今夜、家で一人でいたくないです。
She doesn't want to be alone at home tonight.
polite negative want; doesn't want to be placed in a particular situation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
チームはこの試合だけは負けたくないです。
The team doesn't want to lose this particular match.
targeted reluctance; だけは = only this one specific thing I don't want
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
今日は残業したくないですか？
Don't you want to work overtime today?
polite inquiry about desire to avoid overtime
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
子供の頃、彼はパイロットになりたかったです。
When he was a child, he wanted to become a pilot.
past dream; typical childhood aspiration phrased in polite past
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
山田さんは学生のとき、海外に留学したかったです。
Yamada-san wanted to study abroad when she was a student.
past unfulfilled desire; implies it didn't happen
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
子供の頃、何になりたかったですか？
What did you want to become as a child?
classic retrospective question about childhood dreams
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
姉は本当はあの大学には進学したくなかったですが、親に勧められました。
My older sister didn't really want to go to that university, but her parents recommended it.
本当は = 'deep down / actually'; flags reluctant compliance with external pressure
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
彼らはそのことを誰にも知られたくなかったです。
They didn't want anyone to find out about it.
wanting secrecy; 知られたくない = not wanting to be found out
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
本当はあの仕事、やりたくなかったですか？
Didn't you really not want to do that job?
checking for underlying reluctance beneath a surface compliance
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
いつか自分の店を持ちたい。
Someday I want to have my own shop.
casual life aspiration; 自分の = one's own, with personal ownership emphasis
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼女は誰にも邪魔されずに集中したい。
She wants to focus without being disturbed by anyone.
wanting uninterrupted concentration; without disturbance from others
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今、一番何をしたい？
What do you want to do most right now?
casual direct question about the most pressing current desire
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
もうあの人と関わりたくない。
I don't want to have anything to do with that person anymore.
strong desire to cut ties; もう adds finality to the rejection
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
彼は今夜、何も考えたくない。
He doesn't want to think about anything tonight.
desire for mental rest; wanting to stop thinking
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
本当にもう会いたくない？
Do you really not want to see them anymore?
checking the finality of a decision to end contact
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
ずっと音楽で生きていきたかった。
I always wanted to make a living through music.
past dream of a music-based life; 〜て生きていく = live by doing X
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
あのとき、彼女ともっと話したかった。
I wanted to talk with her more at that time.
regret about a missed opportunity at a specific moment
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃、本当は何になりたかった？
What did you really want to become as a child?
casual retrospective; 本当は = 'if you're being honest about it'
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
あの試合には出たくなかったが、仲間のために戦った。
I didn't want to play in that match, but I fought for my teammates.
reluctant participation out of loyalty to teammates
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
彼は最後まで諦めたくなかった。
He didn't want to give up until the very end.
persistent refusal to give up until the very end
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
本当はあのとき、行きたくなかった？
Did you really not want to go at that time?
checking for hidden reluctance at a past moment
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
VI Comparison & Degree
  1. AよりBのほうが〜B is more ~ than A
  2. 〜のなかで〜がいちばん〜Among ~, ~ is the most ~
  3. たくさんMany, a lot of, plenty
  4. 〜くらい · 〜ぐらいAbout ~, approximately ~
  5. けっこうQuite, fairly, rather, a good deal
N5
AよりBのほうが〜
(A yori B no hou ga ~)
B is more ~ than A
い-adjective
Polite
B + のほうが + A + より + い-adj + です
Casual
B + のほうが + A + より + い-adj
な-adjective
Polite
B + のほうが + A + より + な-adj + です
Casual
B + のほうが + A + より + な-adj + だ
Question — which is more?
Polite
Noun A + と + Noun B + と　どちらのほうが + ですか
Casual
Noun A + と + Noun B + と　どっちのほうが + ？
About

This grammar structure is used to directly compare two things and state that one has more of a quality than the other. のほうが marks the winner — the side with more of the quality. より marks the baseline — the thing being compared against, roughly equivalent to "than" in English.

A key difference from English: adjectives do not change form. There is no Japanese equivalent of "faster" or "cheaper." A い-adjective stays in its plain form; a な-adjective also stays in its plain stem form. Register and tense are expressed by what follows — だ vs です, or past でした — not by modifying the adjective itself.

The より clause can be dropped when the comparison baseline is obvious from context. この店のほうがいいです — "this store is better" — works without stating what it is better than.

For questions, どちら is the standard polite form meaning "which of the two." どっち is its casual contraction. Both take the listing pattern A と B と — と here is not a case particle but a conjunction linking the two options being compared.

Tense and polarity: the predicate conjugates like any normal い-adjective or な-adjective — four forms for each. い-adjective: 安い → 安くない → 安かった → 安くなかった. な-adjective: 便利 → 便利じゃない → 便利だった → 便利じゃなかった. Adding です to any form makes it polite.

Examplesshow ▶
新幹線と飛行機と、どちらのほうが速いですか？
Which is faster, the shinkansen or the plane?
paired comparison question; と〜とどちら = between these two, which?
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
夏より冬のほうが暑くないです。
Winter is not hotter than summer.
counter-intuitive negative comparison; winter is not hotter
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
去年と今年と、どちらのほうが忙しかったですか？
Which was busier, last year or this year?
past comparison between two time periods
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
前のアパートより今のほうが広くありませんでした。
The current apartment isn't bigger than the previous one.
past comparison; current place not bigger than the previous one
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
東京と大阪と、どちらのほうが賑やかですか？
Which is more lively, Tokyo or Osaka?
classic city comparison; な-adj question
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
大阪より東京のほうが有名じゃありません。
Tokyo is not more famous than Osaka.
contradicting the assumed result of a comparison
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
前の会社より今の会社のほうが自由でした。
My current company was more relaxed than my previous one.
past workplace comparison using な-adj でした
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
バスより電車のほうが便利じゃありませんでしたか？
Wasn't the train more convenient than the bus?
past comparison question checking shared assumption
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
夏より秋のほうが旅行のシーズンです。
Autumn is more of a travel season than summer.
season comparison using noun; travel culture framing
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
冬より夏のほうがスキーのシーズンじゃありません。
Summer is not more of a ski season than winter.
obvious negative; stating summer is not the ski season
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
東京より京都のほうが観光地でした。
Kyoto was more of a tourist destination than Tokyo.
past noun comparison; tourism context
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの二人の中で、どちらのほうがリーダーじゃありませんでしたか？
Of those two, which was less of a leader?
asking who was less of a leader of two people
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
コーヒーとお茶と、どっちのほうが苦い？
Which is more bitter, coffee or tea?
casual どっち vs formal どちら; more informal register
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
地下鉄よりバスのほうが速くない。
The bus is not faster than the subway.
plain negative comparison
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
先月より今月のほうが仕事が多かった。
This month had more work than last month.
past comparison of workload between months
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
去年より今年のほうが寒くなかった。
This year wasn't colder than last year.
past comparison; this year was warmer
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
都市より田舎のほうが静かだ。
The countryside is quieter than the city.
general truth comparison; plain casual
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
英語より日本語のほうが難しくない？
Isn't Japanese harder than English?
casual provocative question inviting a debate
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
昨日より今日のほうが元気だった。
I felt better today than yesterday.
daily wellness comparison; past tense
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
前のスマホより今のほうが便利じゃなかった。
My new phone wasn't more convenient than the previous one.
disappointing upgrade; new phone wasn't actually better
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
弟より兄のほうがプロだ。
My older brother is more of a pro than my younger brother.
casual noun comparison; comparing professional levels
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
私より田中さんのほうが専門家じゃない。
Tanaka-san is not more of an expert than me.
claiming more expertise than someone; confident plain assertion
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
あの二人、どちらのほうがリーダーだった？
Of those two, which was more of a leader?
past casual comparison question
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
夏より冬のほうが観光シーズンじゃなかった。
Winter wasn't more of a tourist season than summer.
past negative; winter wasn't the dominant tourist season
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
N5
〜のなかで〜がいちばん〜
(~no naka de ~ga ichiban ~)
Among ~, ~ is the most ~
い-adjective
Polite
Group + の中で + Winner + が　いちばん + い-adj + です
Casual
Group + の中で + Winner + が　いちばん + い-adj
な-adjective
Polite
Group + の中で + Winner + が　いちばん + な-adj + です
Casual
Group + の中で + Winner + が　いちばん + な-adj + だ
Question — which is the most?
Polite
Group + の中で + Q-word + が　いちばん + い-adj / な-adj + ですか
Casual
Group + の中で + Q-word + が　いちばん + い-adj / な-adj + ？
About

This grammar structure is used to express a superlative — picking the one thing out of a group that has the highest degree of a quality. の中で sets the group being compared ("among ~"), が marks the winner as the subject, and いちばん (literally "number one") works as a degree word meaning "most." Like with より comparisons, the adjective stays in its plain form unchanged.

The group can be a single noun (食べ物の中で), a demonstrative (この中で、その中で), or a list of items joined with と (ロシアとフランスと日本の中で). When context is clear, の中で can also be dropped entirely — 秋がいちばん好きです works without stating the group.

For questions, the subject slot is replaced with a question word. Which word to use depends on what you are asking about:

なに what — asking about a thing in general
どれ which one — selecting from a specific visible set
どこ which place — asking about location
だれ who — asking about a person
いつ when — asking about a time or period

Tense and polarity: the predicate conjugates like any normal い-adjective or な-adjective — four forms for each. い-adjective: いちばん高い → 高くない → 高かった → 高くなかった. な-adjective: いちばん有名 → 有名じゃない → 有名だった → 有名じゃなかった. Adding です to any form makes it polite.

Examplesshow ▶
三つのコースのなかで、どれがいちばん難しいですか？
Of the three courses, which is the most difficult?
superlative question over a defined set of options
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
三つのコースのなかで、このコースがいちばん難しくありません。
Of the three courses, this one is not the most difficult.
polite negative superlative; this one is the least difficult of the set
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
今まで食べた料理のなかで、どれがいちばん美味しかったですか？
Of all the food you've eaten, which was the most delicious?
past superlative question; asking about a lifetime food experience
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今年の夏のなかで、先週がいちばん暑くありませんでした。
Of this summer, last week was not the hottest week.
past; that specific week was not the hottest of the summer
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
家族のなかで、誰がいちばん真面目ですか？
In your family, who is the most diligent?
asking who is most diligent within a family group
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
四季のなかで、夏がいちばん好きじゃありません。
Of the four seasons, summer is my least favourite.
least favorite season; 好き + じゃない with superlative framing
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
働いた職場のなかで、前の会社がいちばん自由でした。
Of all the workplaces I've worked at, the previous company was the most relaxed.
most relaxed workplace of all jobs ever held
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
受けた授業のなかで、どれがいちばん有名じゃありませんでしたか？
Of all the classes you took, which was the least well-known?
asking which class was least well-known from all taken
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
スポーツのなかで、野球がいちばん人気です。
Among sports, baseball is the most popular.
broad category superlative using noun; 人気 = popularity
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
チームのなかで、田中さんがいちばんリーダーじゃありません。
In the team, Tanaka-san is not the most of a leader.
among team members, Tanaka is the least leader-like
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
候補者のなかで、誰がいちばん適任者でしたか？
Of the candidates, who was the most suitable?
past superlative across a set of candidates
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
このプロジェクトのなかで、最初の会議がいちばん問題じゃありませんでした。
Of everything in this project, the first meeting was not the biggest problem.
first meeting was not the biggest problem in the project
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
家族のなかで、誰がいちばん背が高い？
In your family, who is the tallest?
casual height comparison; common family conversation
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
三つのルートのなかで、この道がいちばん近くない。
Of the three routes, this road is not the closest.
plain; this route is not the closest of the three
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
人生のなかで、あの夏がいちばん楽しかった。
In my life, that summer was the most fun.
life superlative; carries emotional weight
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今まで住んだ街のなかで、あそこがいちばん住みやすくなかった。
Of all the cities I've lived in, that place was not the most liveable.
most unliveable among all cities lived in
い-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
クラスのなかで、彼がいちばん真剣だ。
In the class, he is the most serious.
sincere/earnest quality; plain present superlative
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
仕事のなかで、何がいちばん好きじゃない？
Of everything at work, what do you like least?
least favorite aspect of work; plain casual
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
今まで飼ったペットのなかで、あの猫がいちばん元気だった。
Of all the pets I've had, that cat was the most energetic.
most energetic pet ever owned
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
住んだ町のなかで、田舎の町がいちばん便利じゃなかった。
Of the towns I've lived in, the rural one was not the most convenient.
most inconvenient place lived among all towns
な-ADJ PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
このクラスのなかで、誰がいちばんリーダーだ？
In this class, who is the most of a leader?
casual direct superlative within a defined group
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
チームのなかで、田中さんがいちばんリーダーじゃない。
In the team, Tanaka-san is not the most of a leader.
plain negative; not the go-to leader on the team
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
働いたプロジェクトのなかで、あれがいちばんチャンスだった。
Of all the projects I worked on, that one was the best opportunity.
best opportunity among all projects worked on
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
候補者のなかで、彼がいちばん適任者じゃなかった。
Of the candidates, he was not the most suitable.
least suitable candidate of all
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
N5
たくさん
(takusan)
Many, a lot of, plenty
before verb
たくさん + Verb
before noun
たくさん + の + Noun
About

This grammar structure is used to express a large quantity or degree — "a lot," "many," or "plenty." Unlike English, Japanese does not use adjective forms like "many" or "much" in the same way. たくさん is technically classified as a noun used adverbially, meaning it does not conjugate at all — it works the same in casual and polite speech, past or present.

When たくさん comes before a verb or predicate, it modifies the action directly — no connector needed. When it comes before a noun, の is inserted between them (たくさんの猫 — a lot of cats). Dropping の is possible but shifts the nuance: without の, たくさん feels like it is describing the whole scene rather than the specific noun.

Word order is flexible when an object is involved. Both 水をたくさん飲む and たくさん水を飲む are natural — たくさん can sit before or after the を-marked object without changing the meaning.

The kanji form 沢山 exists but in everyday writing たくさん in hiragana is standard.

Examplesshow ▶
田中さんは毎日たくさん練習しています。
Tanaka-san practises a lot every day.
ongoing quantity; たくさん modifies the amount of an ongoing action
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
毎日たくさん練習していますか？
Do you practise a lot every day?
asking about practice volume in a polite ongoing context
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
最近、たくさん眠れていません。
I haven't been sleeping much recently.
sleep deprivation; 眠れていない = unable to sleep (potential + progressive)
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
最近あまりたくさん食べていませんか？
Haven't you been eating much recently?
concerned question about not eating enough recently
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週、家族とたくさん話しました。
I talked a lot with my family last week.
quantity of conversation; bonding time with family
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
夏休みにたくさん旅行しましたか？
Did you travel a lot during summer break?
asking about summer travel volume
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
試験前は緊張して、たくさん食べませんでした。
I was nervous before the exam and didn't eat much.
explaining reduced appetite before an exam
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日はあまりたくさん寝られませんでしたか？
Weren't you able to sleep much yesterday?
concern about sleep quantity or quality
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼女は毎晩たくさん読む。
She reads a lot every evening.
habitual reading quantity; nightly habit
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
毎日たくさん歩いてる？
Do you walk a lot every day?
checking daily walking habit
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
最近、彼はたくさん話さない。
Lately he doesn't talk much.
noting someone has become noticeably quieter lately
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
あまりたくさん食べてないの？
Aren't you eating much?
concerned casual question about eating habits
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
学生の頃、たくさんアルバイトをした。
I worked a lot of part-time jobs when I was a student.
multiple part-time jobs; very common student experience in Japan
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃、たくさん泣いた？
Did you cry a lot as a child?
casual question about childhood crying tendencies
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
昨日は疲れて、たくさん動かなかった。
I was tired yesterday and didn't move much.
causal: tired so reduced movement and activity
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
先週はあまりたくさん寝なかった？
Didn't you sleep much last week?
checking for sleep deficit in the previous week
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
この図書館にはたくさんの本があります。
This library has a lot of books.
たくさんの directly modifies a noun; different from adverbial たくさん
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この近くにたくさんの店がありますか？
Are there a lot of shops near here?
asking about density of shops in a neighborhood
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この町にはたくさんの観光地がありません。
There aren't many tourist spots in this town.
town has few tourist attractions; relevant for travel advice
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
あの学校にはたくさんの生徒がいませんか？
Aren't there many students at that school?
expecting a large student body but confirming with mild surprise
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日の試合にはたくさんの観客が来ました。
A lot of spectators came to yesterday's match.
reporting event attendance in polite past
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
そのイベントにたくさんの人が来ましたか？
Did a lot of people come to that event?
asking about event attendance
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
以前はこの辺にたくさんの木がありませんでした。
There weren't many trees around here before.
past landscape; fewer trees before development
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
昔はこの街にたくさんの店がありませんでしたか？
Weren't there many shops in this town before?
historical commercial landscape question
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
冷蔵庫の中にたくさんの食材がある。
There are a lot of ingredients in the fridge.
stocked fridge; plain casual observation
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日のパーティーにたくさんの人が来る？
Will a lot of people come to today's party?
anticipating party attendance with casual question
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
今の若者にはたくさんの選択肢がない。
Young people today don't have many options.
social commentary; young people facing constrained choices
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
この辺にたくさんの駐車場ない？
Aren't there many car parks around here?
casual; looking for nearby parking options
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
あの頃、彼女にはたくさんの夢があった。
Back then, she had a lot of dreams.
past aspirations; nostalgic reflection on someone's former dreams
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃、家にたくさんのおもちゃがあった？
Did you have a lot of toys at home as a child?
casual childhood question about toy ownership
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
子供の頃、家にたくさんの本がなかった。
When I was a child, there weren't many books at home.
few books in childhood home; background for explaining current habits
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
昔、この辺にたくさんの家がなかった？
Weren't there many houses around here before?
asking about past residential density of a neighborhood
NOUN PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
〜くらい · 〜ぐらい
(~kurai · ~gurai)
About ~, approximately ~
quantity
Number / Counter + くらい
question
どの + くらい
About

This grammar structure is used to express an approximate quantity, amount, or duration — equivalent to "about" or "approximately" in English. It attaches directly after a number or counter word. The speaker is not stating an exact figure, just a rough estimate.

くらい and ぐらい are completely interchangeable in meaning. ぐらい is more common in casual spoken Japanese; くらい is slightly more formal and more frequent in writing. Either is acceptable at N5 level.

どのくらい is the standard way to ask "about how much" or "about how long." It can be followed by a particle depending on what is being asked — どのくらいで (how long until), どのくらい alone as an adverb, and so on.

One limit to know: くらい works best with specific or countable extents. For vague, broad time periods ("around spring," "around that era") ごろ is used instead. However, when a specific time point is involved, くらい and ごろ can overlap — 3時くらい and 3時ごろ both mean "around 3 o'clock."

Examplesshow ▶
バス停まで5分くらいかかります。
It takes about 5 minutes to the bus stop.
time estimate; very common when giving directions or describing transit
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
空港までどのくらいかかりますか？
About how long does it take to the airport?
asking for a duration or distance estimate
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この作業は10分くらいでは終わりません。
This task won't be done in about 10 minutes.
more than ~10 minutes needed; では sets the approximate limit
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
30分くらいでは終わりませんか？
Won't it be done in about 30 minutes?
checking if completion falls within about 30 minutes
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日は3時間くらい勉強しました。
I studied for about 3 hours yesterday.
past duration estimate for studying; common self-report
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先週の会議、どのくらい続きましたか？
About how long did last week's meeting go on?
asking approximate duration of a meeting
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
会議は1時間くらいで終わりませんでした。
The meeting didn't finish in about an hour.
meeting ran longer than an hour; took more than expected
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
2時間くらいでは終わりませんでしたか？
Didn't it finish in about 2 hours?
confirming that the task took more than approximately 2 hours
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
毎朝30分くらい走る。
I run for about 30 minutes every morning.
casual daily exercise duration estimate
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
毎日どのくらい歩いてる？
About how much do you walk every day?
casual daily walking distance question
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
彼はあと1時間くらいでは帰らない。
He won't be back for about another hour.
won't be back for about another hour; plain estimation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
あと10分くらいでは着かない？
Won't it arrive in about another 10 minutes?
asking if arrival is imminent; checking rough timing
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
昨日、友達と4時間くらい話した。
I talked with a friend for about 4 hours yesterday.
casual past; estimating duration of a social conversation
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃、毎日どのくらい外で遊んだ？
About how long did you play outside every day as a child?
asking about childhood outdoor play duration
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
試合は2時間くらいで終わらなかった。
The match didn't end in about 2 hours.
game ran longer than roughly 2 hours
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
準備に1時間くらいかからなかった？
Didn't the preparation take about an hour?
checking if preparation took around an hour
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
けっこう
(kekkou)
Quite, fairly, rather, a good deal
adverb
けっこう + Verb / Adjective / Phrase
set phrase
けっこうです
About

This grammar structure is used as a degree adverb meaning "quite," "fairly," or "rather" — expressing that something is more than expected or more than average, but short of extreme. It modifies the entire phrase that follows it directly, without any connector such as な or だ. Although technically classified as a な-adjective, it almost never appears with な — its natural role is adverbial.

けっこう often carries a nuance of mild surprise — that something turned out to be more than the speaker assumed. けっこう難しい implies "it's harder than I expected." This sets it apart from neutral degree words and gives it a slightly evaluative feel.

けっこうです is a completely separate set expression used to politely decline an offer. When someone offers you something and you say けっこうです, it means "No thank you" or "I'm fine, thanks." Context is everything — the same words either praise the degree of something or politely refuse it.

Examplesshow ▶
毎日けっこう歩いています。
I walk quite a lot every day.
けっこう = 'quite a bit / more than you might think'; positive usage for quantity
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この路線はけっこう混みますか？
Does this line get quite crowded?
checking if a train line gets notably crowded
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨夜はけっこう飲みました。
I drank quite a lot last night.
admitting to drinking more than usual; けっこう has a 'more than expected' nuance
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんは昨日のパーティーでけっこう飲みましたか？
Did Tanaka-san drink quite a lot at yesterday's party?
asking about someone else's intake at an event
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
この辺の家賃はけっこう高いです。
The rent around here is quite high.
casual observation about notably high rent
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
新しいオフィスはけっこう遠いですか？
Is the new office quite far?
checking if distance is notable or surprising
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週の試験はけっこう難しかったです。
Last week's exam was quite difficult.
acknowledging difficulty; けっこう is softer than とても or すごく
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先月のプロジェクトはけっこう長かったですか？
Was last month's project quite long?
checking if a project's duration was notably long
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
田中さんはけっこう真面目です。
Tanaka-san is quite diligent.
qualified positive assessment; けっこう = 'actually quite'
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この仕事はけっこう大変ですか？
Is this job quite tough?
assessing work difficulty with a checking tone
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日の会議はけっこう大変でした。
Yesterday's meeting was quite tough.
reporting past difficulty in polite past
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
入社した頃はけっこう不安でしたか？
Were you quite anxious when you first started?
asking about early-career anxiety level
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼はけっこう食べる。
He eats quite a lot.
casual observation; he's a notably big eater
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
あの店、けっこう並ぶ？
Does that place have quite a long queue?
asking about queue length; food culture context in Japan
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
昨日はけっこう走った。
I ran quite a lot yesterday.
casual past; ran more than usual
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昨日の練習、けっこう疲れた？
Were you quite tired from yesterday's practice?
checking if training was notably tiring
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
この問題はけっこう難しい。
This problem is quite difficult.
plain; not easy, turns out it's actually somewhat hard
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
その本、けっこう厚い？
Is that book quite thick?
checking if a book is notably thick (i.e., long)
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
あの映画はけっこう長かった。
That film was quite long.
retrospective; movie ran longer than expected
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昨日の宿題、けっこう難しかった？
Was yesterday's homework quite difficult?
casual check on homework difficulty
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
彼女はけっこう有名だ。
She is quite famous.
qualified fame; not hugely famous but fairly well-known
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
この近くのラーメン屋、けっこう有名？
Is the ramen shop near here quite well-known?
casual local reputation check
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
子供の頃、彼はけっこう元気だった。
When he was a child, he was quite energetic.
past energy level; 'he was actually pretty lively as a kid'
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
大学時代、けっこう暇だった？
Were you quite free during your university days?
asking about free time during student days
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
VII Giving & Receiving
  1. あげるTo give — away from speaker
  2. くれるTo give — toward speaker
  3. もらうTo receive — from someone
N5
あげる
(ageru)
To give — away from speaker
Polite
Giver は · が
+
Recipient に
+
Object を
+ あげます
Casual
Giver は · が
+
Recipient に
+
Object を
+ あげる
About

This grammar structure is used to express giving when the transfer goes away from the speaker — either the speaker gives something to someone else, or one third party gives to another third party. The critical constraint: あげる is never used when the recipient is the speaker or someone psychologically close to the speaker. That direction uses a different verb.

Three participants, three particles: the giver takes は or が, the recipient takes に, and the object takes を. Word order is flexible — the particles carry the meaning, not the position. All three arrangements in the examples below express the same thing.

The kanji form 上げる exists but in the giving sense あげる is written in hiragana in everyday contexts.

Examplesshow ▶
私は田中さんにこのカメラをあげます。
I'll give this camera to Tanaka-san.
standard giving; speaker gives outward to a third party; あげる flows away from speaker
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんにお土産をあげますか？
Are you going to give Tanaka-san a souvenir?
asking if someone will give a souvenir to a specific person
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼にはもうプレゼントをあげません。
I'm not giving him any more presents.
withholding future gifts; は emphasizes the contrast
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
本当に彼に何もあげませんか？
Are you really not going to give him anything?
surprised confirmation of a refusal to give anything
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
先週、後輩に教科書をあげました。
Last week I gave my junior a textbook.
giving downward to a junior; あげる can go up or down in social hierarchy
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんは誰にそのバッグをあげましたか？
Who did Tanaka-san give that bag to?
asking about the recipient of a specific gift
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
結局、彼には何もあげませんでした。
In the end, I didn't give him anything.
withheld all gifts; final report with 結局 = 'in the end'
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
田中さんは本当に何もあげませんでしたか？
Did Tanaka-san really not give anything?
surprised disbelief at the withheld gifts
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
明日、彼女にこの本をあげる。
I'll give her this book tomorrow.
plain casual future giving
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
チームのみんなにお菓子をあげるの？
Are you going to give everyone on the team sweets?
curious about who the sweets are for; の adds seeking-explanation nuance
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
あの人には絶対あげない。
I'm definitely not giving it to that person.
emphatic refusal; 絶対 = definitely, absolutely not
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
弟にはあげないの？
You're not going to give it to your brother?
casual surprise that a sibling is excluded from the giving
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
後輩に古いパソコンをあげた。
I gave my junior my old laptop.
passing down old equipment to a junior; common generous act
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
誰にチケットをあげたの？
Who did you give the tickets to?
casual inquiry about the recipient of tickets
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
結局、誰にも何もあげなかった。
In the end, I didn't give anything to anyone.
gave nothing to anyone in the end; total withholding
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
なんであの人にあげなかったの？
Why didn't you give it to that person?
questioning a decision not to give to a specific person
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
くれる
(kureru)
To give — toward speaker
Polite
Giver は · が
+
Recipient に
+
Object を
+ くれます
Casual
Giver は · が
+
Recipient に
+
Object を
+ くれる
About

This grammar structure is used to express giving when the transfer goes toward the speaker — someone gives something to the speaker, or to someone emotionally close to the speaker such as a family member or close friend. The critical constraint: くれる is never used when both the giver and recipient are outside the speaker's psychological in-group — people the speaker has no personal connection to. That direction uses あげる.

The particle structure is the same as あげる: the giver takes は or が, the recipient takes に, and the object takes を. In practice, the recipient (私に) is almost always dropped — it is obvious from context that the speaker is the one receiving. The giver can also be omitted when clear from context, leaving just the object and くれる.

Examplesshow ▶
課長はいつも有益なアドバイスをくれます。
Our section chief always gives us useful advice.
superior gives to speaker or in-group; くれる marks this inward direction
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんが毎回お土産をくれますか？
Does Tanaka-san give you souvenirs every time?
checking if Tanaka consistently gives souvenirs to you or your group
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
彼はいつも約束したものをくれません。
He never gives me what he promised.
lamenting someone never delivers on promised things
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
もう少し時間をくれませんか？
Won't you give me a little more time?
politely asking for more time; くれない + か = polite request
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
部長が退職祝いに高い時計をくれました。
The department head gave me an expensive watch as a retirement gift.
gift received from a superior; polite past
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
誰がこれをくれましたか？
Who gave this to you?
asking who gave you something you received
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
入院中、誰もお見舞いをくれませんでした。
Nobody gave me anything when I was in hospital.
no one visited or brought anything while hospitalized; resentful or sad
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
田中さんも何もくれませんでしたか？
Tanaka-san didn't give you anything either?
checking if even Tanaka failed to give anything
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
おばあちゃんは会うたびにお小遣いをくれる。
My grandmother gives me pocket money every time we meet.
regular affectionate giving from grandmother; plain habitual
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼氏、誕生日に何かくれる？
Is your boyfriend going to give you something for your birthday?
asking about an expected gift from a boyfriend
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
彼は何もくれない。
He never gives me anything.
plain complaint; he never gives anything
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
これ、くれない？
Can you give me this?
casual request using くれない as 'won't you give me this?'; softer than ちょうだい
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
友達が引っ越し祝いに観葉植物をくれた。
My friend gave me a houseplant as a housewarming gift.
housewarming gift; casual past; 引っ越し祝い is a set phrase
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
これ、誰がくれたの？
Who gave you this?
casual origin question about a received item
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
誰も何もくれなかった。
Nobody gave me anything.
nobody gave anything; total absence of generosity
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
彼女、何もくれなかったの？
She didn't give you anything?
casual disappointed question; expectation unmet
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
もらう
(morau)
To receive — from someone
Polite
Recipient は · が
+
Giver に · から
+
Object を
+ もらいます
Casual
Recipient は · が
+
Giver に · から
+
Object を
+ もらう
About

This grammar structure is used to express receiving something from someone. The focus is on the recipient, not the giver. Unlike あげる and くれる where に marks the recipient, in もらう the particles flip: は or が marks the recipient (the one performing the act of receiving), and に or から marks the giver.

Both に and から can mark the giver. に feels more personal and is used when there is a direct human relationship involved. から emphasises the source or origin — "from" in the sense of a point of departure — and can be used with institutions or less personal sources.

As with くれる, the recipient is often omitted when it is the speaker and obvious from context. The giver can also be dropped when clear or irrelevant to the point being made.

Examplesshow ▶
毎年、会社から手帳をもらいます。
I receive a planner from the company every year.
annual gift from institution; regular occurrence framing
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
誕生日に何をもらいますか？
What do you receive for your birthday?
asking about birthday gifts; very common social exchange
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
会社の人からは贈り物をもらいません。
I don't accept gifts from people at work.
not accepting gifts from colleagues; implies an ethical policy
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
本当に何ももらいませんか？
Are you really not going to accept anything?
surprised at a principled refusal to accept anything
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
上司に記念品をもらいました。
I received a commemorative gift from my boss.
received a gift from a superior; に marks the giver with もらう
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
誰から花束をもらいましたか？
Who did you receive that bouquet from?
asking about the source of flowers received
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今年は誰からも年賀状をもらいませんでした。
I didn't receive any New Year's cards from anyone this year.
no New Year's cards received from anyone; sad or notable absence
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
田中さんからも何ももらいませんでしたか？
You didn't receive anything from Tanaka-san either?
checking if even Tanaka sent nothing
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
毎週、親からお小遣いをもらう。
I receive pocket money from my parents every week.
regular pocket money from parents; plain habitual
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
これ、誰からもらうの？
Who are you getting this from?
casual source question; の adds a seeking-explanation nuance
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
知らない人からは何ももらわない。
I don't take anything from strangers.
safety rule; never take anything from strangers
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
本当にもらわないの？
You're really not going to take it?
casual surprised push-back at the refusal
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
卒業式で先生に花をもらった。
I received flowers from my teacher at the graduation ceremony.
classic graduation milestone; receiving flowers from a teacher
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
これ、誰からもらったの？
Who did you get this from?
casual past source question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
結局、誰からも何ももらわなかった。
In the end, I didn't receive anything from anyone.
received nothing from anyone in the end; total absence
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
本当に何ももらわなかったの？
Did you really not receive anything?
casual question; the の adds slight disbelief or seeking explanation
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
VIII Time & Sequence
  1. 〜前にBefore ~
  2. 〜てからAfter doing ~ · Once ~ is done
  3. からFrom ~ · Because ~
  4. までUntil ~ · Up to ~ · As far as ~
  5. Verb + までUntil (something) happens
  6. Time boundariesrecap
N5
〜前に
(~mae ni)
Before ~
before doing — temporal
Verb dictionary form
+ 前に
About

This grammar structure is used to express that one action happens before another. The verb directly before 前に is always in the dictionary form, even when the main verb of the sentence is in the past. The subordinate action described by 前に has not yet happened at the time of reference — so past tense is not used there regardless of the overall sentence tense.

Examplesshow ▶
寝る前に、本を読みます。
I read a book before going to bed.
前に always takes the dictionary form of the verb regardless of the main clause tense
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
出かける前に、窓を閉めますか？
Do you close the windows before going out?
checking a habitual pre-departure routine
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
食べる前に、手を洗いません。
I don't wash my hands before eating.
stating a bad habit plainly; not framed as an excuse
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
寝る前に、スマホを見ませんか？
Don't you look at your phone before going to bed?
rhetorical observation or mild criticism about phone use at bedtime
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
日本に来る前に、一学期日本語を勉強しました。
I studied Japanese for one semester before coming to Japan.
the 前に verb stays in dictionary form even when the main clause is past
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
決める前に、誰かに相談しましたか？
Did you consult anyone before deciding?
past tense shows the pre-action window has already passed
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
提出する前に、内容を確認しませんでした。
I didn't check the content before submitting.
implies negligence; the expected preparatory step was skipped
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
会議が始まる前に、資料を送りませんでしたか？
Didn't you send the materials before the meeting started?
negative question implying mild rebuke or surprise at the omission
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
家に入る前に、靴を脱ぐ。
I take off my shoes before entering the house.
cultural norm stated as a personal habit; plain casual observation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
寝る前に、何か食べる？
Do you eat anything before going to bed?
casual check on someone's bedtime eating habit
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
走る前に、ストレッチしない。
I don't stretch before running.
stating a personal habit in plain style; not self-critical
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
出かける前に、鍵をかけない？
Don't you lock up before going out?
casual reminder framed as a question; gentle prompt
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
引っ越す前に、近所に挨拶した。
I greeted the neighbours before moving out.
stating compliance with common social etiquette before moving
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
辞める前に、誰かに相談した？
Did you talk to anyone before quitting?
past question; slightly incredulous or concerned
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
寝る前に、歯を磨かなかった。
I didn't brush my teeth before going to bed.
casual admission of skipping a routine
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
サインする前に、内容を読まなかったの？
You didn't read it before signing?
の at the end adds exasperation or disbelief at the omission
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
〜てから
(~te kara)
After doing ~ · Once ~ is done
Verb [て] te form
+ から + Main clause
About

This grammar structure is used to express that one action is fully completed before another begins — equivalent to "after doing A, B" or "once A is done, B." The emphasis is on A enabling or unlocking B.

It can also describe a state that has continued since A happened — 猫が死んでから、さびしいです.

Critical distinction: 食べてから = after eating; 食べるから = because I will eat; 食べたから = because I ate. Only the て-form + から gives "after."

Examplesshow ▶
仕事が終わってから、電話します。
I'll call you after work is done.
sequential: the call happens only after work; 〜てから emphasizes order matters
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
帰ってから、何をしますか？
What will you do after getting home?
open inquiry about post-return plans
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
薬を飲んでから、お酒を飲みません。
I don't drink alcohol after taking medicine.
medical caution stated as a general rule
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
確認してから、送りませんか？
Why don't we send it after checking?
cautious suggestion; implies do it properly before sending
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
東京に引っ越してから、とても忙しくなりました。
After moving to Tokyo, I became very busy.
〜てから + なった marks a life change and its consequence
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
結婚してから、料理を始めましたか？
Did you start cooking after getting married?
asking about a life change triggered by marriage
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
転職してから、給料が上がりませんでした。
After changing jobs, my salary didn't go up.
disappointment; the expected positive result didn't follow the change
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
日本語を勉強してから、全然使いませんでしたか？
After studying Japanese, didn't you use it at all?
rhetorical; implies the study effort was wasted
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
シャワーを浴びてから、朝ごはんを食べる。
I eat breakfast after taking a shower.
fixed personal morning routine; 〜てから signals non-negotiable sequence
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
帰ってから、何する？
What are you going to do after getting home?
casual post-return plans question
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
ご飯を食べてから、すぐ寝ない。
I don't go to sleep right after eating.
health or habitual preference stated plainly
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
卒業してから、仕事を探さないの？
Aren't you going to look for work after graduating?
mild pressure or surprise about post-graduation plans
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
大学を卒業してから、日本に来た。
After graduating university, I came to Japan.
life timeline; 〜てから marks a clear before and after
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
日本に来てから、日本語が上手くなった？
Has your Japanese improved since coming to Japan?
checking on improvement since a key life moment
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
子供が生まれてから、全然眠れなかった。
After the baby was born, I couldn't sleep at all.
expressing the impact of parenthood; 〜てから marks the triggering life change
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
引っ越してから、近所の人に挨拶しなかったの？
You didn't introduce yourself to the neighbours after moving in?
の marks mild reproach; failing a social expectation
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
N5
から
(kara)
From ~ · Because ~
Spatial / Temporal
starting point Noun
+ から
Reason — Because
verb / い-adj
plain form Verb
/ い-adj + から
な-adj / noun
な-adj / Noun + だ + から
About

This grammar structure is used to mark a starting point in space or time, or to express a reason. The underlying meaning is always "from" — from a location, from a time, or from a reason (because).

From (spatial · temporal): attaches directly to a place or time noun. No だ is added — doing so would shift the meaning to "because." Often paired with まで to mark a full range: 9時から5時まで (from 9 to 5).

Because (reason): the reason clause comes first, followed by から and then the result clause. Verbs and い-adjectives attach directly in plain form. Nouns and な-adjectives require だ before から in the present affirmative only.

The reason clause before から can also use polite form in formal speech. から is softer and more subjective than ので, which carries a more formal and objective tone.

Examplesshow ▶
Spatial / Temporal
東京から大阪まで新幹線で行きます。
I'll go from Tokyo to Osaka by shinkansen.
origin + destination pair; から/まで often appear together
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
毎朝どこから通勤していますか？
Where do you commute from every morning?
asking for the point of origin in a commute
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日は朝から晩まで働きました。
Yesterday I worked from morning to night.
朝から晩まで is an idiomatic fixed phrase meaning all day long
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
どこから歩いてきましたか？
Where did you walk from?
asking for the starting point of a walk
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
9時から会議が始まる。
The meeting starts from 9.
start time; から signals the opening boundary of an event
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
何時から開いてる？
What time does it open from?
casual; omits subject; very common spoken question
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
子供の頃からずっと日本語を勉強した。
I've been studying Japanese since childhood.
〜頃から marks a fuzzy starting period rather than an exact time
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昨日のコンサート、何時から始まった？
What time did yesterday's concert start?
topic-first sentence structure common in spoken Japanese
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
Reason — Because
熱があるから、学校を休みます。
Because I have a fever, I'll skip school.
causal から here signals reason, not physical origin
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日は田中さんが来るから、準備しませんか？
Since Tanaka-san is coming today, why don't we get ready?
〜から + negative suggestion = 'given that X, let's...'
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
雨が降っているから、出かけない。
Because it's raining, I won't go out.
direct causal reasoning; plain casual everyday logic
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
知らないから、何も言えない。
Because I don't know, I can't say anything.
chain of plain clauses; casual, not rude
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
疲れたから、早く寝た。
Because I was tired, I went to bed early.
past cause connects to past outcome; common personal anecdote frame
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昨日食べなかったから、お腹が空いた。
Because I didn't eat yesterday, I got hungry.
negative cause leading to an outcome; simple chain
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
この店は高いから、あまり行きません。
Because this shop is expensive, I don't go much.
i-adj + から; no だ needed before から with い-adjectives
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
遠くないから、歩いて行きます。
Because it's not far, I'll walk.
negative i-adj + から; no くないだから needed
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
このケーキは甘いから、好きじゃない。
Because this cake is sweet, I don't like it.
casual preference stated with causal reason
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は暑いから、プールに行く？
It's hot today — shall we go to the pool?
offering a reason then suggesting an outing
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
高くないから、毎日食べる。
Because it's not expensive, I eat it every day.
because it's affordable; plain everyday logic
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
昨日は寒かったから、家から出なかった。
Because it was cold yesterday, I didn't leave the house.
past cause + past result; natural spoken chain
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
田中さんは真面目だから、任せられます。
Because Tanaka-san is diligent, I can leave it to them.
な-adj + だ + から in polite form; full explicit copula
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの頃は暇だったから、よく旅行しました。
Because I had free time back then, I traveled a lot.
past な-adj uses だったから; polite register
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この店は有名だから、いつも混んでいる。
Because this shop is famous, it's always crowded.
na-adj plain: 有名だ + から; full copula before から
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
この仕事は大変じゃないから、誰でもできる。
Because this job isn't hard, anyone can do it.
negative な-adj + から; no extra particle needed
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
そんなに複雑じゃないから、自分でできる？
Since it's not that complicated, can you manage it yourself?
negative na-adj reason + question suggesting self-sufficiency
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
子供の頃は元気だったから、毎日外で遊んだ。
Because I was energetic as a child, I played outside every day.
past casual chain reflecting on energetic childhood
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は日曜日だから、店が閉まっています。
Because today is Sunday, the shops are closed.
noun + だから in polite context; explaining a state of affairs
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
来週は試験だから、今日一緒に勉強しませんか？
Since next week is the exam, why don't we study together today?
upcoming event used as justification for an invitation
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
昨日は祝日だったから、銀行が休みでした。
Because yesterday was a national holiday, the bank was closed.
past noun + だったから; explaining why something was closed
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
明日は休みだから、映画を見に行こう。
Because tomorrow is a day off, let's go see a movie.
reason → volitional suggestion; casual weekend planning
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は仕事じゃないから、ゆっくりする。
Because today isn't a workday, I'll take it easy.
noun negation + から; explaining relaxed plans
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
学生だったから、お金がなかった。
Because I was a student, I had no money.
past noun cause for a past outcome; common personal anecdote frame
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
まで
(made)
Until ~ · Up to ~ · As far as ~
endpoint — time / location / quantity
Noun + まで
range — from ~ to ~
Noun A + から + Noun B + まで
About

This grammar structure is used to mark the endpoint of time, location, or quantity. It is the natural counterpart to から, and the two are frequently paired: から marks the starting point, まで marks the end.

The noun before まで determines the type of endpoint: a time word gives "until that time," a location noun gives "as far as that place," and a number or quantity gives "up to that amount."

Examplesshow ▶
Endpoint — time / location / quantity
駅まで歩きます。
I'll walk as far as the station.
destination limit; まで can mark the physical endpoint of movement
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
何時まで働きますか？
How late do you work?
asking for the time limit of an activity
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
五時まで会社を出ません。
I won't leave the office until five.
negative + まで = 'won't leave until'; deadline construction
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
十二時まで働きました。
I worked until midnight.
past; worked up to midnight as the endpoint
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
締め切りまで諦めませんでした。
I didn't give up until the deadline.
persevered all the way to the deadline
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
終電まで飲む。
I'll drink until the last train.
colloquial; drinking until the last train is a common Tokyo social phrase
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
どこまで歩く？
How far are you walking?
casual; asking the extent or endpoint of walking
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
六時まで起きない。
I won't get up until six.
won't get up before 6; まで + negative = 'not until'
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
百まで数えた。
I counted up to a hundred.
counting up to a numerical limit
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
朝まで眠れなかった。
I couldn't sleep until morning.
couldn't sleep at all until the morning came
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
Range — から～まで
月曜日から金曜日まで、毎日日本語を勉強します。
I study Japanese every day from Monday to Friday.
から/まで pair for a time span; Monday through Friday
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
月曜日から何曜日まで休みですか？
From Monday, what day are you off until?
asks for the end boundary of a time span
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
うちから会社まで、電車で一時間かかりました。
It took an hour from home to the office by train.
distance frame + duration; common commute description pattern
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
東京から大阪まで新幹線で二時間半かかる。
It takes two and a half hours from Tokyo to Osaka by shinkansen.
standard factual statement about transit time between two cities
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
何時から何時まで開いてる？
What hours is it open?
casual inquiry about full opening hours
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
月曜日から金曜日まで、お酒は飲まない。
I don't drink alcohol from Monday to Friday.
self-imposed rule for a defined time span
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
大阪から東京まで車で走った。
I drove all the way from Osaka to Tokyo.
emphasizes the full physical extent of the journey
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
朝から晩まで何も食べなかった。
I didn't eat anything from morning to night.
朝から晩まで is idiomatic for 'all day'; emphasizes total deprivation
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
Noun Predicate
学校は五時までです。
School is until five o'clock.
stating the closing time of an institution; noun + まで
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
締め切りは今日までじゃありません。
The deadline isn't today.
correcting a mistaken assumption about a deadline
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
夏休みは八月まででした。
Summer break was until August.
past; summer break continued until August
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日は夜九時までだ。
Today it's until nine in the evening.
elliptical; 'it' (shift, event, or session) lasts until 9pm
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は何時まで？
Until what time today?
casual elliptical; asks how late something goes without stating the subject
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
受付は五時までじゃない、六時までだ。
Reception isn't until five — it's until six.
correction of a mistaken assumption about reception hours
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
N5
Verb + まで
(Verb + made)
Until (something) happens
Verb dictionary form
+ まで、 + Main clause
About

This grammar structure is used to express that Clause B continues until the event described in Clause A occurs. The verb before まで is always dictionary form regardless of the overall sentence tense. Tense is expressed in the predicate of Clause B.

If Clause A and Clause B have different subjects, が marks the subject of Clause A: 赤ちゃんが寝るまで、歌を歌ってあげます.

Examplesshow ▶
晴れるまで、喫茶店でコーヒーを飲みながら待ちます。
I'll wait in the café drinking coffee until it clears up.
waiting until a natural event occurs; combines まで with ながら
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
電車が来るまで、どこで待ちますか？
Where will you wait until the train comes?
practical question about how to spend the waiting period
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
日本語が上手になるまで、国に帰りません。
I will not go back to my country until I become fluent in Japanese.
strong personal resolve; the goal is the condition for returning home
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
先生が来るまで、教室で静かに待っていました。
I waited quietly in the classroom until the teacher came.
polite past scene-setting; describes waiting behavior at a reference time
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
漢字が読めるまで、新聞は読めませんでした。
I couldn't read newspapers until I could read kanji.
describes a learning barrier that has since been overcome
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
雨が止むまで、ここで待つ。
I'll wait here until the rain stops.
casual resolved waiting; plain form for natural event
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼女が来るまで、何してる？
What are you doing until she gets here?
casual check on how someone is filling the wait
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
仕事が終わるまで、帰らない。
I'm not going home until the work is done.
firm resolve or workplace commitment; not leaving until done
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
友達が来るまで、駅をウロウロした。
I wandered around the station until my friend came.
