ウロウロ is an onomatopoeic word for aimless wandering; casual past
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
田中さんが教えてくれるまで、全然わからなかった。
I had no idea until Tanaka-san explained it to me.
expresses complete ignorance that was resolved by someone's explanation
VERB PAST NEGATIVE PLAIN
Quick Recap
Time boundaries
前に · てから · から · まで
Comparison
Form Attaches to Function Feels like
〜前に Verb (dictionary form) / Noun + の One action happens before another "Before ~"
〜てから Verb (て form) One action happens only after another is completed "After ~ / Once ~ is done"
から Noun (time / place) Starting point of an action or span "From ~"
まで Noun (time / place / quantity) / Verb (dictionary form) Endpoint — the action continues up to that limit "Until ~ / Up to ~"
When to use what
Doing something before another action or event → 〜前に
Doing something after — and only once — another action is finished → 〜てから
Marking where or when something starts → から
Marking where or when something stops — or until an event happens (verb + まで) → まで
Full span in one breath — from A to B → から〜まで
前に / てから order two actions; から / まで mark the boundaries of a single one
IX Scope & Temporal Adverbs
  1. だけOnly, Just
  2. もうAlready · Anymore · One more
  3. まだStill · Not yet
  4. まだ〜ていませんStill haven't done ~
  5. もう vs まだrecap
N5
だけ
(dake)
Only, Just
Verb
Verb dictionary form
+ だけ
い-adj
い-adj + だけ
な-adj
な-adj + な + だけ
Noun
Noun + だけ
About

This grammar structure is used to limit or restrict scope to only one thing, person, or action — equivalent to "only" or "just" in English. The implication is that other possibilities exist but are excluded.

Attachment is straightforward for verbs (dictionary form), い-adjectives, and nouns — だけ attaches directly. For な-adjectives, な is required before だけ when the adjective is used as a quality. If the な is dropped, the な-adjective is treated as a noun instead, which slightly shifts the nuance: 綺麗なだけ = "just beautiful (as a quality)"; 綺麗だけ = "just beauty (as a concept/thing)".

Examplesshow ▶
コーヒーを飲むだけにします。
I'll just have a coffee.
polite restraint; 'just coffee for me'; verb + だけ limits the action
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
見るだけでいいですか？
Is it okay to just look?
common shop phrase meaning 'just browsing'; 見るだけ is a fixed expression
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
写真を撮るだけにしました。
I just took some photos — that was it.
decided to limit oneself to photos only; implies temptation to do more
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
この映画は長いだけで、面白くないです。
This movie is just long — it's not interesting.
i-adj + だけで = 'merely'; the only quality it has, implying no redemption
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女は有名なだけで、実力はありません。
She's just famous — she has no real skill.
な-adj + な + だけで; dismissive of hollow fame without substance
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんだけが知っています。
Only Tanaka-san knows.
only Tanaka knows; が marks the exclusive subject in this structure
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
コーヒーだけでいいですか？
Is just coffee okay?
checking if coffee alone is sufficient for the guest
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
お金だけでは幸せになれません。
You can't be happy with just money.
では emphasizes money alone is insufficient; philosophical or emphatic tone
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
姉だけが合格しました。
Only my older sister passed.
only my sister passed; implies the others did not
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今夜は食べるだけ、何も飲まない。
Tonight I'm only going to eat — I won't drink anything.
self-restriction from drinking while still eating tonight
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
見るだけ？
Just looking?
casual echo question; slight skepticism ('really just looking?')
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
少し話すだけにした。
I just had a quick chat — that was it.
limited the interaction; didn't want it to become a long conversation
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
このスープは辛いだけ。
This soup is just spicy — that's all it is.
dismissive flat criticism; the only attribute is spiciness
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
安いだけ？味はどう？
Just cheap? What about the taste?
interrogating if cheapness is the only positive; probing quality
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
あのレストラン、高かっただけだった。
That restaurant was just expensive — nothing more.
retrospective disappointment; high price was its only distinguishing feature
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
この店は便利なだけで、料理はいまいちだ。
This place is just convenient — the food is mediocre.
convenience is the only positive; food is mediocre
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼は有名じゃないだけで、実力はある。
It's just that he's not famous — he has real skill.
negating the adjective flips the implication; he has skill but lacks recognition
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
子供の頃は素直だっただけ。
As a child I was just obedient — that's all.
reflecting on a past quality in a distancing way
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
私はブロッコリーだけがきらい。
I only dislike broccoli.
only broccoli; が marks the one exception from everything else
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
それだけ？
Is that all?
それだけ = 'is that all?'; common expression of surprise or disappointment
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
努力だけじゃ足りない。
Effort alone isn't enough.
では condensed to じゃ in casual; effort alone isn't the complete answer
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
彼女だけが合格した。
Only she passed.
only she passed; plain version of a selective outcome
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
もう
(mou)
Already · Anymore · One more
already
もう +
Phrase past tense
anymore
もう +
Phrase negative
one more
もう + Number / Counter
About

This grammar structure is used to express that something has already happened or is no longer the case. The literal meaning is "something has unexpectedly ended" — which is why it maps to "already" in past sentences and "anymore" in negative ones.

In positive-past sentences, もう indicates the action was completed sooner than expected. In negative sentences, it indicates a state that was ongoing has now ended. In questions, the speaker suspects something may have ended and wants to confirm.

もう also has a completely separate usage with numbers and counters, meaning "one more" or "a little more" — もう一度 (one more time), もう少し (a little more), もう一枚 (one more sheet). Context always makes clear which meaning is intended.

もう is the opposite of まだ. もう signals something has ended; まだ signals it is still ongoing.

Examplesshow ▶
Already
もう宿題をしました。
I already did my homework.
もう + past = 'already done'; often said to reassure or preempt the question
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう着きましたか？
Have you already arrived?
checking if someone has already arrived; slight surprise or eagerness
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
朝ごはんはもう食べた。
I already ate breakfast.
casual confirmation of already having eaten; topic は marks the subject
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
もうデザートを食べた？
Did you already eat dessert?
surprised or curious inquiry about early dessert
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
Anymore
もうタバコを吸いません。
I don't smoke anymore.
もう + negative present = 'no longer'; quit statement
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
もうテレビは見ませんか？
Don't you watch TV anymore?
asking if someone has given up TV; polite curiosity
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
もう彼女に会わない。
I won't see her anymore.
strong final statement; not 'not yet' but 'no longer / never again'
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
もう戻らない？
Aren't you coming back?
casual question; checking if someone is definitely not coming back
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
もうお金がない。
I don't have money anymore.
casual complaint; もう here adds exasperation to the 'no money' state
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
One More
もう一度言ってください。
Please say it one more time.
もう一度 = 'one more time'; very common polite request for repetition
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう少し待ってください。
Please wait a little longer.
もう少し = 'a little more'; requesting a small additional wait
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう一杯飲みますか？
Will you have one more drink?
offering another round; もう一杯 is a fixed phrase in social settings
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
もう少し考えたい。
I want to think a little more.
expressing desire for a little more time to consider a decision
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
もう一枚いる？
Do you need one more?
casual offer; もう一枚 = one more sheet or piece
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
ビールをもう一本頼んだ。
I ordered one more bottle of beer.
ordered another; もう一本 is a set counter phrase for cylindrical items
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
まだ
(mada)
Still · Not yet
ている
まだ +
Verb [て] te form
+ いる
いる
まだ + Noun + が + いる
ある
まだ + Noun + が + ある
About

This grammar structure is used to describe a state that is still ongoing — equivalent to "still" in English. まだ itself simply flags that the state is continuing; the verb tense controls whether it reads as "still" (positive) or "not yet" (negative).

With ている it describes an action or condition that is actively continuing. With が + いる or が + ある it describes a person, animal, or thing that is still present or still exists. まだ with a negative verb ("not yet") is covered in the next card.

まだ can also be used in questions when the speaker thinks something might still be ongoing and wants to confirm — まだ日本語の勉強しているの？ (Are you still studying Japanese?)

まだ is the opposite of もう. When emphasising that something is nowhere near finished or ready, まだ is often doubled — まだまだ — meaning "still a long way to go" or "not nearly there yet."

Rare kanji form: 未だ.

Examplesshow ▶
まだ仕事をしています。
I'm still working.
ongoing activity that hasn't ended; implies the expected end hasn't come yet
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ練習していますか？
Are you still practicing?
checking if an ongoing activity continues; slight surprise possible
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
まだ雨が降っていました。
It was still raining.
past continuous still happening at a past reference time
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ田中さんがいます。
Tanaka-san is still here.
someone still present; often implies they haven't left as expected
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ先生がいますか？
Is the teacher still here?
checking if someone is still around; common in school or office context
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
まだ時間があります。
There's still time.
reassuring that time remains; often said under pressure
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
まだ席がありますか？
Are there still seats available?
checking for remaining availability; common in restaurants or theaters
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
まだ走っている。
I'm still running.
present ongoing; implies the activity is longer than expected
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだ起きている？
Are you still up?
late-night check; surprised someone is still awake
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
まだ彼女のことを考えていた。
I was still thinking about her.
reflective past; still thinking about her at a certain moment
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだ親がいるからゲームできない。
My parents are still here, so I can't play games.
causal chain: parents are still here → can't play games; waiting for them to leave
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだ猫がいる？
Is the cat still here?
checking on the cat's continued presence; casual
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
まだ宿題があるから、遊ばない。
I still have homework, so I can't hang out.
prioritizing due to continuing obligation
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだお金がある？
Do you still have money?
checking if funds are still available; often with mild concern
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
まだ問題があった。
There were still problems.
discovering that problems were still present at a past reference time
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだまだだね。
You still have a long way to go.
まだまだ is a fixed expression; used as honest or playful feedback on progress
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
まだ〜ていません
(mada ~te imasen)
Still haven't done ~
Polite
まだ +
Verb [て] te form
+ いません
Casual
まだ +
Verb [て] te form
+ いない
About

This grammar structure is used to say something is still not done — the action has not occurred yet as of now, but may still happen. It focuses on the current state of incompletion rather than the past. This is close to the English present perfect negative ("haven't done X yet").

It combines まだ ("not yet") with the ている construction in its negative form. まだ sets the expectation that the action is pending; ていない · ていません describes the state of it not having happened.

Critical distinction — まだ〜ていません vs 〜ませんでした: まだ〜ていません is strictly for things that are still incomplete at the present moment. It cannot be used for completed past events. If something did not happen at a specific point in the past and that moment is now over, use the simple past negative 〜ませんでした instead.

私はまだ宿題をしていません。 Present incomplete — may still do it
私はきのう宿題をしませんでした。 Past negative — event is finished
Examplesshow ▶
私はまだ宿題をしていません。
I haven't done the homework yet.
polite 'not yet'; states current incomplete status
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
スーさんはまだ起きていません。
Sue hasn't woken up yet.
third-party report that someone hasn't woken up yet; neutral
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
その映画はまだ見ていません。
I still haven't watched that movie.
haven't seen it yet; common when someone recommends a movie
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
私はまだ昼ご飯を食べていません。
I haven't eaten lunch yet.
explaining hunger or lateness for lunch
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
まだ終わっていませんか？
Haven't you finished yet?
slight impatience or surprise at ongoing incompletion
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE QUESTION
まだ返事をしていない。
I haven't replied yet.
casual; admitting delay in replying
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
まだシャワーを浴びていない。
I haven't showered yet.
casual admission; implying about to do it or explaining current state
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
まだ彼に話していない。
I haven't told him yet.
haven't told him yet; often implies procrastination or avoidance
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
まだ決めていない？
Haven't you decided yet?
checking on someone's indecision; can be impatient or encouraging
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
Quick Recap
もう vs まだ
もう · まだ · まだ〜ていません
Comparison
Form With affirmative With negative Feels like
もう Already — the change has happened (もう食べた) Anymore — the state has ended (もう食べない) "Already" / "Not anymore"
まだ Still — the situation continues (まだ食べている) Not yet — it hasn't happened (まだ食べていない) "Still" / "Not yet"
まだ〜ていません — The standard polite way to say an action hasn't happened yet "Still haven't ~"
When to use what
It has happened or the change is complete → もう + affirmative
It used to be the case but no longer is → もう + negative
The situation is still going on → まだ + affirmative
It hasn't happened yet, but is expected to → まだ〜ていません
Mirror logic: もう marks a change, まだ marks no change — polarity decides the English: already / not anymore / still / not yet
X Core Particles
  1. は · がTopic marker · Subject marker
  2. をDirect object marker
  3. に · へDestination · Location · Time · Direction
  4. に + 行く · 来る · 帰るTo go · come · return in order to ~
  5. でPlace of action · Means · Method
  6. のPossession · Description · Nominalizer
  7. もAlso · Too · Either · Both
  8. と · やAnd (exhaustive) · With · And (non-exhaustive)
  9. が · けど · けれどもBut · However — contrast & concession
  10. かQuestion marker · Or
  11. よ · ねSentence-final particles — assertion · agreement
N5
は · が
(wa · ga)
Topic marker · Subject marker
subject
Subject + が + Verb / Adjective
topic
Topic + は + Comment
About は

は is a particle that marks the topic of the sentence — what the whole sentence is "about." The topic is always something the speaker assumes is already present in the listener's mind. The emphasis falls on what comes after は — the new information or comment. The particle is written は but pronounced わ (wa).

Once a topic is established, it can be dropped in subsequent sentences — Japanese avoids unnecessary repetition.

Contrast use: は has a secondary function of signalling comparison when it appears mid-sentence on something other than the main topic.

About が

が marks the grammatical subject. It surfaces when the subject is new, unknown, or being specifically identified or contrasted. Because が identifies the subject as the specific answer to "who" or "what," it emphasizes what comes before it — the core contrast with は.

New subject · identification: when answering a だれ or なに question, が must be used. Once established, subsequent sentences naturally shift to は.

は vs が

は emphasizes what comes after it — the comment. が emphasizes what comes before it — the subject being identified. The two particles can coexist in the same sentence doing different jobs.

Words that always require が

Certain verbs and adjectives require が to mark what they relate to: いる、ある、わかる、できる、見える、聞こえる、好き、きらい、上手、下手、欲しい、必要、大切。

Examplesshow ▶
は — topic
東京は大きいです。
Tokyo is big.
は marks topic; neutral general statement about Tokyo
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
魚は好きです。肉は嫌いです。
I like fish. I dislike meat. (contrastive は)
contrastive は; each は draws contrast with the other preference
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
カレーは辛い。
Curry is spicy.
plain casual general truth; は used for a known fact about curry
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
が — subject
富士山が見えます。
Mt. Fuji can be seen.
が marks the visible subject; perceptual verbs like 見える take が
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
公園に猫がいます。
There is a cat in the park.
が marks the existent subject; 猫 is new information introduced into the scene
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
近所の子供たちが走る。
The neighbourhood kids are running.
が marks the specific subjects doing the action; observed scene
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
は vs が — contrast
田中さんはコーヒーを飲みます。
Tanaka drinks coffee. (は = Tanaka is the topic)
は makes Tanaka the topic; could contrast with other people
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
田中さんがコーヒーを飲みます。
It's Tanaka who drinks coffee. (が = specific identification)
が identifies Tanaka specifically; answers the question 'who?'
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
猫が好き。
I like cats. (が = emphatic, strong feeling)
strong emotional preference; が often used with feelings and abilities
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
猫は好きだけど、犬は苦手。
I like cats, but dogs not so much. (contrastive は)
double contrastive は; comparing two preferences by setting each as a topic
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
を
(wo)
Direct object marker
Noun + を + Verb
About

を is a particle that marks the direct object of a verb — the thing being acted upon. The particle is written を but pronounced お (o). を is never dropped and cannot be replaced by any other particle in this role.

Movement through a place: を also marks a place that an action passes through or moves along: 公園を歩く (walk through a park). This is distinct from に (destination) or で (location of action).

Examplesshow ▶
Direct object
パンを食べます。
I eat bread.
basic transitive object marker; を marks the direct object
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
肉を食べません。
I don't eat meat.
negative; を is retained even in negation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
映画を見ました。
I watched a movie.
polite past transitive action
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
毎日音楽を聴く。
I listen to music every day.
聴く specifically for intentional listening, unlike 聞こえる for passive hearing
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
テレビをあまり見ない。
I don't watch TV much.
あまり + negative = 'not very much'; を retained in negation
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
Movement through space
公園を散歩します。
I take a walk through the park.
を marks the space traversed; に would mark the destination
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
公園を歩く。
I walk through the park.
を marks the path or space moved through, not a destination
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
廊下を走った。
I ran through the hallway.
を used for movement through or along a space; common with 走る、飛ぶ、歩く
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
に · へ
(ni · e)
Destination · Location · Time · Direction
destination / direction
Place + に / へ + Motion Verb
specific time
Time + に + Verb
indirect object / recipient
Person + に + Verb
existence location
Place + に + がある / がいる
About に

に always marks a fixed endpoint or target — a specific point in space, time, or relation that the action reaches or is anchored to.

Destination: with motion verbs (行く, 来る, 帰る, 入る, 乗る), に marks the arrival point: 学校に行く.

Existence location: いる and ある always use に to mark where something exists. に marks where something is (fixed point); で marks where an action takes place.

Indirect object · recipient: を marks the direct object; に marks the receiver or direction of the verb's effect: 母にプレゼントをあげた, 先生に質問をしました.

に with time — required, forbidden, optional

Required: exact numbered time references — clock times, specific dates, days of the week, years.

Forbidden: relative time expressions (きのう、あした、今日、まいにち、いま、とき) reject に entirely.

Optional: parts of the day and seasons allow but often omit に.

About へ

へ marks direction or goal of movement with motion verbs. に specifies the arrival point precisely; へ is more abstract — "toward." に is more common in everyday speech.

Examplesshow ▶
Destination
学校に行きます。
I go to school.
に marks destination; more precise and common than へ for destinations
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
日本へ来ました。
I came to Japan.
へ marks general direction; slightly more literary than に for destinations
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
駅に向かう。
I'm heading to the station.
に with 向かう; marks the target direction
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Time
六時に起きます。
I wake up at 6 o'clock.
に marks specific time points for actions; clock times always use に
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
七時に寝た。
I went to bed at 7.
specific bedtime; に used for clock times
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Indirect object
母にプレゼントをあげました。
I gave my mother a present.
に marks recipient of giving; に + あげる/もらう/くれる is a fixed pairing
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先生に質問します。
I ask the teacher a question.
に marks the person directed at; 質問する is always paired with に
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
友達にメッセージを送る。
I send a message to my friend.
に marks the recipient; 送る takes に for the person receiving
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Location of existence
部屋に猫がいます。
There is a cat in the room.
に marks location of existence; います and あります always take に
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
机の上に本がある。
There's a book on the desk.
に marks location of existence; ある/います always take に for location
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
へ — direction
家へ帰ります。
I'm going home.
へ sounds more directional and homeward; に also works but へ feels natural here
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
東京へ行く。
I'm going to Tokyo.
へ for casual direction statement; interchangeable with に but slightly softer
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
に + 行く · 来る · 帰る
(ni+iku · kuru · kaeru)
To go · come · return in order to ~
to go
Place + に + Verb stem + に + 行く
to come
Place + に + Verb stem + に + 来る
to return
Place + に + Verb stem + に + 帰る
About

This grammar structure is used to express going, coming, or returning somewhere for the purpose of doing something — equivalent to "go/come/return in order to [verb]" in English. It describes the intention behind the movement, not just the movement itself.

There are two に particles in this structure. The first に marks the destination (place you are heading to). The second に marks the purpose (the action you intend to do there). The verb before the second に must be in its stem form — the form you get by removing ます. Using the full ます-form here is incorrect.

The destination particle に can be replaced by へ with a slight nuance shift: に emphasises the destination as an endpoint, while へ emphasises the direction of travel or the journey itself.

Examplesshow ▶
行く — to go
山へ写真を撮りに行きます。
I'm going to the mountain to take pictures.
verb stem + に + motion verb; purpose expressed as a noun-like stem
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
どこに食べに行きますか？
Where are you going to eat?
asking about the destination of a purpose trip
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は映画を見に行きません。
I'm not going to see a movie today.
not going for that purpose today; polite negative
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
スーパーに野菜を買いに行きました。
I went to the supermarket to buy vegetables.
went specifically to buy; に marks both destination and purpose
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
公園に走りに行く。
I'm going to the park for a run.
going to run; the park is both destination and context
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
何しに行く？
What are you going there for?
casual; what are you going there for?
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
海に泳ぎに行った。
I went to the beach to swim.
casual past; went to swim; に marks purpose and destination
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は買い物に行かない。
I'm not going shopping today.
買い物 is already a noun so に行かない works directly without a stem
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
来る — to come
先生が説明しに来ます。
The teacher is coming to explain.
someone coming to you with a purpose; 来る used when motion is toward speaker
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
何時に迎えに来ますか？
What time are you coming to pick me up?
迎えに来る is a fixed expression meaning 'to come and pick someone up'
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は手伝いに来ません。
I'm not coming to help today.
not coming for that purpose today; 手伝い is noun form
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
ともだちがあそびに来ました。
My friend came to hang out.
friend visiting for leisure; あそびに来る is a common phrase
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
弟が遊びに来る。
My younger brother is coming to play.
expected near-future visit from a sibling
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
何しに来るの？
What are you coming here for?
slightly suspicious or curious; の adds a seeking-explanation nuance
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
彼が謝りに来た。
He came to apologize.
came to apologize; purpose of coming is the key information
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は遊びに来ない。
He's not coming to hang out today.
negation of a recurring visit; 'not coming to hang out today'
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
帰る — to return
荷物を取りに帰ります。
I'm going back to get my luggage.
帰る = return home; purpose is retrieval of an item left behind
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
昼に家に食べに帰りますか？
Are you going home for lunch?
going home midday for lunch; common practice in Japan
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
今日は着替えに帰りません。
I'm not going home to change today.
not going home to change; implies staying at work or out
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
家に書類を取りに帰りました。
I went back home to get the documents.
went back specifically to retrieve documents
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
犬に餌をあげに帰る。
I'm heading home to feed the dog.
returning home for a specific care task
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
財布を取りに帰った。
I went back to get my wallet.
forgot wallet and had to return; very relatable situation
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昼に家に食べに帰らない。
I'm not going home for lunch.
negative; not going home for lunch today
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
N5
で
(de)
Place of action · Means · Method
place of action
Place + で + Action Verb
tool / means / method
Tool / Means + で + Verb
About

で marks the context of an action — where it happens, how it is done, or with what it is done. It always marks something required for the action to take place.

Place of action: で marks the location where a physical or intentional action takes place: 学校で勉強する, レストランで食べた. The place is not a destination — it is the context the action requires.

Tool · instrument · method: で marks the means used: はしで食べます, ペンで書きます.

Transportation · communication: バスで行きます (by bus), 日本語で話しましょう (in Japanese).

で vs に: に marks where something goes or exists. で marks where an action happens. Existence verbs いる and ある always use に, never で.

Examplesshow ▶
Location of action
学校で勉強します。
I study at school.
で marks location of an action; 学校に would mark destination or arrival
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
レストランで食べました。
I ate at the restaurant.
location of eating; で not に because eating is the activity, not movement
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
家で仕事をする。
I work at home.
working from home; で marks the activity location
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
図書館で本を読んだ。
I read a book at the library.
typical study or reading location
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Means / method
はしで食べます。
I eat with chopsticks.
で marks instrument or means; chopsticks as the tool used
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
バスで行きます。
I go by bus.
で marks mode of transport; always で for vehicles and means of travel
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
自転車で来た。
I came by bike.
came by bicycle; で marks the mode of getting there
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Language / medium
日本語で話しましょう。
Let's speak in Japanese.
で marks the language medium; switching to Japanese for the conversation
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
英語でメールを書く。
I write emails in English.
writing in a specific language uses で; the language is the medium
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Scope / limit
三人でやる。
We'll do it with the three of us.
三人で = 'with the three of us'; で marks group composition
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
の
(no)
Possession · Description · Nominalizer
nominalizer
Verb plain form
+ の + は / が
possession / description
Noun A + の + Noun B
About の — possession / description

の links two nouns, where the first noun modifies or describes the second. The second noun is always the main idea. This covers possession (わたしの本), origin (日本の車), category (日本語の本), and description (白いシャツの人).

の as pronoun substitute

When the noun is clear from context, の can stand in for it: これはわたしのです (This is mine), 赤いのを買います (I'll buy the red one).

のは / のが — nominalizer

の can follow a verb phrase in plain form to turn it into a noun. The nominalized phrase then functions as topic (のは) or subject (のが): 日本語を話すのが好きです. のが is the natural partner for 好き, 嫌い, 上手, 下手.

Examplesshow ▶
Possession / belonging
これは田中さんの本です。
This is Tanaka-san's book.
basic possession; の links possessor to possessed noun
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
これは私のだ。
This is mine.
pronoun + の as a standalone noun meaning 'mine'
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Noun modification
日本の車
A Japanese car.
noun modifier; の links an attribute to the following noun
NOUN
白いシャツの人
The person in the white shirt.
description + の + noun; defining the person by what they're wearing
NOUN
Nominalization の
日本語を話すのが好きです。
I like speaking Japanese.
の nominalizes the verb clause, making it the subject of 好き
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
音楽を聴くのは楽しいです。
Listening to music is fun.
の nominalizes the clause; は marks the topic for the following comment
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
一人で食べるのは寂しい。
Eating alone is lonely.
nominalizing allows the action to be the topic; sets up an emotional comment
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Standalone の
はい、これはわたしのです。
Yes, this is mine.
standalone の as possessive pronoun; avoids repeating the noun
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
N5
も
(mo)
Also · Too · Either · Both
also / too / either
Noun + も + Verb / Adjective
both A and B
Noun A + も + Noun B + も + Verb / Predicate
About

も signals inclusion — whatever is true for A is also true for B. も replaces は, が, or を entirely — the original particle is dropped. も always builds on prior context.

Negative sentences: in a negative sentence, も shifts meaning from "also" to "either" or "neither": 私も行きません.

Double も — both A and B: も can appear twice when two items share the same verb and grammatical role: パンもたまごも食べました.

Examplesshow ▶
Also / too
映画を見ました。ドラマも見ました。
I watched a movie. I also watched a drama.
も adds the second item to the first; inclusive addition
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
兄は医者です。姉も医者です。
My brother is a doctor. My sister is also a doctor.
も replaces は to show 'also'; confirms a shared attribute
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女も来る。
She's coming too.
も replaces が; she too, in addition to others
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Either / neither
彼女も来ませんでした。
She didn't come either.
も + negative = 'didn't either'; inclusive negation
VERB PAST NEGATIVE POLITE
私も行かない。
I'm not going either.
I'm not going either; も + negative = inclusive exclusion
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
田中さんも山田さんも来ない。
Neither Tanaka nor Yamada is coming.
double も + negative = 'neither...nor'; emphatic double exclusion
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
Both A も B も
コーヒーもお茶も好きです。
I like both coffee and tea.
double も = 'both...and'; inclusive affirmation of two likes
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
公園に猫がいます。犬もいます。
There is a cat in the park. There is also a dog.
も replaces が to add another existing entity to the scene
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
猫も犬も好き。
I like both cats and dogs.
double も in plain casual; I like both
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
料理も洗濯もする。
I both cook and do laundry.
double も listing tasks; doing both, implying there may be more
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
と · や
(to · ya)
And (exhaustive) · With · And (non-exhaustive)
with — accompaniment
Person + と + Verb
and — exhaustive list
Noun A + と + Noun B
and — non-exhaustive list
Noun A + や + Noun B + （や）
About と

Exhaustive list: と connects nouns into a complete group — implying nothing has been left out. と appears after each noun; the final と is often dropped in conversation.

Accompaniment: と marks who someone does an action together with. Both parties are mutually involved — distinguishing it from に: 先生と話す (talk with the teacher — mutual) vs 先生に話す (talk to the teacher — one-directional).

About や

や connects nouns in a non-exhaustive list — the items are examples from a larger set. と vs や: と = complete list. や = representative sample. ビールとワインを飲んだ — only those two. ビールやワインを飲んだ — among other drinks.

Examplesshow ▶
と — complete listing
りんごとバナナを買いました。
I bought apples and bananas (just those two).
と lists an exhaustive pair; exactly these two and nothing else
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
消しゴムとペンと鉛筆がある。
There is an eraser, a pen, and a pencil.
exhaustive list of three; と between each item
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
や — partial listing
京都や奈良に行きました。
I went to Kyoto and Nara (among other places).
や implies a non-exhaustive list; went to those places among others
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
本やざっしやマンガを読みました。
I read books, magazines, manga, and so on.
や list of reading materials; 'and so on' is implied
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
と — with
友だちと映画を見ました。
I watched a movie with my friend.
と = together with; marks the companion in a shared activity
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
家族と話す。
I talk with my family.
talking with family; と marks mutual or shared action
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
と vs や / と vs に
鞄にペンと財布と鍵を入れた。
I put a pen, wallet, and keys in my bag — exactly those.
exhaustive と list; exactly those three items, no more
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
鞄にペンや財布を入れた。
I put a pen, wallet, and other things in my bag.
や implies other things also went in; not a complete list
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
先生と話します。
I talk with my teacher.
と = with; mutual talking together with the teacher
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先生に話します。
I talk to my teacher.
に = to; unidirectional speech directed at the teacher
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
N5
が · けど · けれども
(ga · kedo · keredomo)
But · However — contrast & concession
Most formal
verb / い-adj
Verb / い-adj + が、 + Clause B
な-adj / noun
な-adj / Noun + だ + が、 + Clause B
Formal
verb / い-adj
Verb / い-adj + けれども + Clause B
な-adj / noun
な-adj / Noun + だけれども + Clause B
Casual
verb / い-adj
Verb / い-adj + けど + Clause B
な-adj / noun
な-adj / Noun + だけど + Clause B
About

This grammar structure connects two clauses with a contrasting or softening relationship — "A, but B" or "A; however, B." Clause A states something, and Clause B introduces a point that qualifies, contrasts, or adds to it. The contrast can be strong or soft — all three forms can also soften a statement rather than express direct opposition.

All three forms follow the same attachment rules: verbs and い-adjectives attach directly. な-adjectives and nouns require だ before the conjunction.

Register scale: the same conjunction exists across a full formality range — けど → けども → けれど → けれども → が (most formal). けど is casual, natural with friends and family. けれども suits polite speech. が is the most formal and direct — common in written Japanese and formal statements.

Trailing が: が can appear at the very end of a sentence with no Clause B, leaving the implication deliberately unstated: まあ、綺麗ですが。

Examplesshow ▶
が — most formal
毎日走るが、運動はきらい。
I run every day, but I don't like exercise.
が = but in plain formal or literary style; slightly stiff in casual speech
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
今日は行かないが、明日は行く。
I'm not going today, but I'll go tomorrow.
が connects two contrasting facts; plain formal
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
昨日食べたが、まだ腹が減っている。
I ate yesterday, but I'm still hungry.
past + present contrast; が bridges two temporally distinct clauses
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
このカレーは辛いが、美味しい。
This curry is spicy, but delicious.
concession: spicy but still good; が highlights the contradiction
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
安くないが、買う価値はある。
It's not cheap, but it's worth buying.
not cheap but worth it; counterbalancing a negative with a positive
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
試験は難しかったが、合格した。
The exam was hard, but I passed.
past adversity overcome; が is typical in written or narrative style
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
車は便利だが、危ない。
Cars are convenient, but dangerous.
な-adj + だが; balanced observation, slightly formal register
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼は有名じゃないが、実力はある。
He's not famous, but he has real skill.
contrast between public recognition and actual ability
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
昔は元気だったが、今は疲れやすい。
I used to be energetic, but I get tired easily now.
temporal contrast using past vs present; narrative register
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼は先生だが、とても若い。
He is a teacher, but very young.
noun + だが; presents a surprising or unexpected follow-up
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼は学生じゃないが、毎日図書館にいる。
He's not a student, but he's at the library every day.
negative noun + が; surprising behavior for someone who isn't a student
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
昔は会社員だったが、今は自営業だ。
I used to be a company employee, but now I'm self-employed.
career change narrative; が bridges the before and after
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
けれども — formal
お酒は飲むけれども、ワインは飲みません。
I drink alcohol, but I don't drink wine.
けれども is more formal than けど; polite register, keeps ます throughout
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日は行きませんけれども、明日は行きます。
I'm not going today, but I'll go tomorrow.
polite version of contrast; stating non-attendance now, attendance later
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日は食べたけれども、まだお腹が空いています。
I ate yesterday, but I'm still hungry.
polite past concession; felt odd still being hungry after eating
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
水が怖いけれども、泳ぐのが好きです。
I'm afraid of water, but I like swimming.
paradoxical; fears water but enjoys swimming
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
高くないけれども、品質は良いです。
It's not expensive, but the quality is good.
polite concession; not expensive but good quality
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日は忙しかったけれども、楽しかったです。
Yesterday was busy, but it was fun.
past polite け concession; busy day but enjoyable
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの店は便利だけれども、少し高いです。
That shop is convenient, but a little pricey.
な-adj + だけれども; polite, slightly formal
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女は親切じゃないけれども、仕事はできます。
She's not particularly kind, but she's capable.
negative な-adj + けれども in polite
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
子供の頃は苦手だったけれども、今は好きです。
I used to be bad at it as a child, but now I like it.
past contrast with present in polite register
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼は学生だけれども、とても忙しいです。
He is a student, but he's very busy.
noun + だけれども; polite observation about age gap
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
先生じゃないけれども、教えるのが上手です。
He's not a teacher, but he's great at explaining things.
negative noun + けれども in polite; surprising talent without the title
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE POLITE
昨日は休みだったけれども、仕事をしていました。
Yesterday was a day off, but I was working.
irony; it was supposed to be a day off but work happened
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
けど — casual
毎日泳ぐけど、今日は泳がない。
I swim every day, but I won't swim today.
けど is the most casual of the three; everyday spoken speech
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
野菜は食べないけど、果物は好き。
I don't eat vegetables, but I like fruit.
casual food preference contrast
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
昨日映画を見たけど、つまらなかった。
I watched a movie yesterday, but it was boring.
casual post-screening disappointment
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
北海道は寒いけど、綺麗だ。
Hokkaido is cold, but beautiful.
casual travel concession; cold but beautiful
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
このラーメン、辛くないけど、おいしくない。
This ramen isn't spicy, but it's not good either.
double negative contrast; neither spicy nor good
い-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
あの映画は長かったけど、面白かった。
That movie was long, but it was interesting.
past; despite its length, it was worth watching
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
田舎は静かだけど、不便だ。
The countryside is quiet, but inconvenient.
countryside trade-off; peaceful but inconvenient
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
あの店は有名じゃないけど、料理はうまい。
That place isn't famous, but the food is great.
hidden gem phrasing; unknown but great food
な-ADJ PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
学生の頃は暇だったけど、今は忙しい。
I had plenty of free time as a student, but now I'm busy.
casual life-stage contrast; student days vs now
な-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
これはステーキだけど、冷たい。
This is a steak, but it's cold.
what it is vs how it is; mild complaint about temperature
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
これは日本語の本じゃないけど、役に立つ。
This isn't a Japanese textbook, but it's useful.
it's not what you'd expect but still useful
NOUN PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
去年は学生だったけど、今は働いている。
I was a student last year, but now I'm working.
personal transition; student last year, working now
NOUN PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
か
(ka)
Question marker · Or
or — choice
Noun A + か + Noun B + （か）
yes/no question
Statement + か
About

か is a particle that functions as a verbal question mark. Placed at the end of a statement, it turns that statement into a yes/no question without changing word order.

Or — choice questions: placed between two or more nouns, か means "or." The options are listed with か after each one; the final か is optional in spoken Japanese.

Uncertainty: か is also used embedded mid-sentence to express that something is unknown: 美味しいか分からない.

Register: か regularly follows です and ます. In casual speech, bare か can sound blunt or demanding: 大丈夫ですか (standard) vs 大丈夫か (very casual).

Examplesshow ▶
Yes / no question
日本に行きますか。
Are you going to Japan?
か at end of polite statement marks a yes/no question
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もう食べましたか。
Have you already eaten?
か on past affirmative = 'have you already done...?'
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
大丈夫か。
You okay? (blunt / masculine)
plain + か is blunt; used among close peers or by seniors to juniors
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
まだ寝てないか。
Haven't you slept yet?
plain negative + か implies checking on someone or mild concern
VERB PRESENT NEGATIVE PLAIN
Choice / or
コーヒーかお茶を飲みますか。
Will you have coffee or tea?
か between nouns = 'or'; offering a choice
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
映画かゲームをしよう。
Let's watch a movie or play games.
か for casual 'or' between options in a suggestion
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
Embedded uncertainty
おいしいか分からない。
I don't know if it's good.
embedded question; か within a clause nominalizes it as 'whether'
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
誰が来るかわからない。
I don't know who will come.
embedded question with a question word; か marks the embedded clause boundary
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
N5
よ · ね
(yo · ne)
Sentence-final particles — assertion · agreement
assertion / emphasis
Sentence + よ
agreement / softening
Sentence + ね
softened assertion
Sentence + よね
About

よ and ね are conversational particles placed at the end of a sentence. They don’t change the core meaning but signal how the speaker wants to engage the listener. They are not interchangeable — each serves a distinct communicative purpose.

よ — assertion & emphasis: よ is used to assert or emphasize information that the speaker believes the listener does not know or needs to hear. It adds a sense of “just so you know” or “I’m telling you.” Used when giving new information or being emphatic. Can sound stronger or more confident than plain statements.

ね — seeking agreement & softening: ね is used when the speaker is looking for agreement, wants confirmation, or wishes to soften a statement. It functions like “right?”, “isn’t it?”, or “you know?” in English. It indicates the speaker expects the listener shares the same view. It may also be pronounced ねえ for added softness.

よね — softened assertion: used together (usually as よね), the two particles combine the assertiveness of よ with the shared-understanding quality of ね. The speaker asserts something but still invites agreement — closer to “right?!” than either particle alone.

Examplesshow ▶
よ — assertion
田中さんはもうオフィスに来ていますよ。
Tanaka-san is already at the office [just so you know].
よ delivers information the listener doesn’t have; polite ます form
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あのシェフ、有名な店で修行していたんですよ。
That chef trained at a famous restaurant [you know].
んですよ — よ after explanatory の(ん) adds informing emphasis to background context
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
このスープ、かなり濃いですよ。
This soup is quite rich [just so you know].
よ used to warn or inform; speaker assumes listener hasn’t tried it yet
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女の提案、かなり的確ですよ。
Her suggestion is quite on point [I’m telling you].
よ asserts the speaker’s evaluation as new information the listener should take note of
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
来週の月曜日は祝日ですよ。
Next Monday is a national holiday [just so you know].
よ delivers a fact the listener may be unaware of; common in everyday reminders
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼、もう知ってるよ。
He already knows [you know].
plain よ; casual assertion — speaker informs listener who apparently doesn’t realize this
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
さっき財布落としたよ。
You just dropped your wallet [you know].
よ used to urgently alert the listener to something they haven’t noticed; plain register
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
その川、思ったより深いよ。
That river is deeper than you think [just so you know].
よ warns of something the listener is underestimating; plain い-adj
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
あの新しい先生、かなり厳格だよ。
That new teacher is quite strict [I’m telling you].
だよ attaches to な-adj; speaker shares insider knowledge in casual register
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
それ、弟のランドセルだよ。
That’s my little brother’s school bag [you know].
だよ on noun; speaker corrects or clarifies the listener’s assumption
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
ね — agreement
毎週末、山を歩いているんですね。
You walk in the mountains every weekend, don’t you.
ね seeks confirmation of something the speaker has observed or heard about
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
もうお昼を食べましたね？
You’ve already had lunch, right?
ね？ in polite form; gentle confirmation with rising intonation
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE QUESTION
このお茶、少し苦いですね。
This tea is a bit bitter, isn’t it.
ね invites a shared reaction; speaker assumes listener has the same impression
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
彼女はとても丁寧ですね。
She’s very polite, isn’t she.
ね softens an observation and invites the listener to share the same view
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
今日はいい天気ですね。
It’s nice weather today, isn’t it.
classic ね usage; shared observation about surroundings, no new information conveyed
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
弟、最近よく練習してるね。
Your brother has been practicing a lot lately, hasn’t he.
plain ね; expresses mild admiration while seeking shared understanding
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
昨日のパーティー、楽しかったね？
Yesterday’s party was fun, right?
plain ね with rising intonation; both attended, speaker seeks confirmation of shared feeling
い-ADJ PAST AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
新しいアパート、けっこう広いね。
The new apartment is pretty spacious, isn’t it.
ね invites agreement on something both can see; plain い-adj
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
新しい職場、なかなか快適だね。
The new workplace is quite comfortable, isn’t it.
だね on な-adj; casual shared assessment of a new environment
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼、新入社員だね？
He’s a new employee, right?
だね？ on noun; plain question checking a shared assumption
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN QUESTION
よね — softened assertion
この電車、新宿に止まりますよね。
This train stops at Shinjuku, right?
よね asserts what the speaker believes while checking the listener agrees
VERB PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
あの報告書、田中さんが書きましたよね。
Tanaka-san wrote that report, right?
よね on past verb; speaker is fairly sure but invites confirmation
VERB PAST AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
このパソコン、けっこう重いよね。
This laptop is pretty heavy, right?
よね in plain; shared physical experience — both are probably holding the device
い-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
あの公園、夜は静かだよね。
That park is quiet at night, right?
だよね on な-adj; asserts shared knowledge and invites agreement
な-ADJ PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE PLAIN
彼女は企画担当ですよね。
She’s in charge of planning, right?
よね on noun; checks a shared understanding politely without full certainty
NOUN PRESENT AFFIRMATIVE POLITE
